- [x] Manually delete `api/chain/notifications` pods of `Development` as needed so they redeploy
- [x] Verify all services (api/chain/notifications, etc.) are healthy and running this version

items.title: Signing from “Ready to sign” tab does not clear “new transaction ready to sign” notification
items.body:
**Description**  
When I sign a multisig transaction from the “Ready to sign” tab, the “new transaction ready to sign” notification is not cleared if my signature alone is not enough to move the transaction from “waiting for signatures” to “waiting for execution”. This leaves a persistent notification for a transaction that I have already signed and can no longer act on, which is confusing and makes it harder to see truly new transactions that still need my attention.

**Steps to reproduce**  
1. Have a multisig transaction in status “waiting for signatures”.  
2. Observe that the “new transaction ready to sign” notification is shown.  
3. Open the “Ready to sign” tab.  
4. Sign the transaction, but do not add enough signatures to move the transaction status from “waiting for signatures” to “waiting for execution”.

**Expected behavior**  
After I sign the transaction from the “Ready to sign” tab, the “new transaction ready to sign” notification for that transaction should be cleared for my account, even if the overall transaction status remains “waiting for signatures”.

**Actual behavior**  
After signing from the “Ready to sign” tab, the “new transaction ready to sign” notification remains visible, even though I have already signed and there is nothing left for me to do on that transaction.

items.title: Settings tab only shows organization-specific settings instead of app-wide settings
items.body:
## Description
The current Settings tab is scoped only to the connected organization, which is confusing for users. When users open Settings, they expect to see all settings relevant to the app as a whole, but instead they only see settings for the currently connected organization. This makes it unclear where to find global app settings and blurs the distinction between app-level and organization-level configuration. 

## Problem
- Settings currently shows only organization-specific options tied to the active organization.
- Users expect a Settings area to contain global app settings (e.g., account, security, appearance, general behavior), not just organization-level configuration.
- Users do not have a clear place to manage organization details separately from app-level configuration.

## Expected Behavior
- The Settings tab should be the central place for app-wide settings.
- Users should be able to quickly see and manage:
- Global app settings (account, security, general preferences, etc.).
- A clear entry point to organization-specific settings/details.
- Organization-specific configuration should be clearly separated from global settings so users understand the scope of each change.

## Actual Behavior
- The Settings tab primarily exposes settings for the currently connected organization.
- Users become confused when they open Settings and only see organization settings, assuming they are looking at all app settings.
- There is no dedicated, clearly labeled place for per-organization details/settings distinct from global app-level configuration.

## Proposed UX / Structural Changes
1. Make the Settings tab the home for app-wide/global settings:
    - Show user/account, security, general app behavior, notifications, etc.
    - Treat this as the “App Settings” area rather than “Organization Settings”.
2. Move organization-specific settings into an Organization Details section/page:
    - Accessible from within Settings (e.g., “Organizations” → select an organizat

items.title: Make notification services resilient to NATS outages
items.body:
**Description**:  
`api/chain/notification` services should not crash or become unavailable if NATS is down or unreachable. They must continue to function, and a backup solution for fan-out messaging (e.g., TCP-based or similar) should be researched and implemented so that notifications can still be delivered when NATS is unavailable.

**Requirements**:  
- Update NATS client usage so that:
  - Service startup does not crash if NATS cannot be reached.  
  - Connection loss triggers graceful degradation instead of process exit (e.g., stop publishing to NATS, but keep HTTP/API endpoints up).
- Implement reconnection and health-handling logic:
  - Use automatic reconnection with backoff and unlimited retries where appropriate.
  - Log NATS connection errors and status changes clearly for operations.
- Research and design a backup fan-out mechanism when NATS is unavailable:
  - Options could include direct TCP connections, database-backed outbox pattern, or another message transport that can be enabled as a fallback.
  - Define how messages are queued and delivered when NATS returns.  
- Define behavior for fan-out when NATS is down:
  - How to avoid message loss.  
  - How to avoid duplicate delivery when NATS comes back and both paths might send.  

**Acceptance criteria**:  
- `api/chain/notification` services remain up and responsive when NATS is unavailable (no crashes or failed container restarts).  
- NATS outages are handled via reconnection logic and clear logs, without blocking core APIs.
- A documented backup fan-out design is agreed upon and an initial implementation is in place (or a concrete plan exists if phased).  
- Fan-out behavior is verified under simulated NATS outage (e.g., stopping the NATS cluster) and recovery, with no data loss and no service crashes.

items.title: Avoid creator auto-signing when exporting hybrid transactions from TTv2
items.body:
**Description**:  
For hybrid signing, TTv2 exports a transaction for signing in TTv1. Because of protobuf serialization/deserialization differences between the Java and JS SDKs, TTv2 must sign the transaction before exporting, so TTv1 treats it as frozen. Currently, TTv2 always signs exports with the creator’s key, which prevents a hybrid fallback path that relies on having no additional signatures. TTv2 must skip signing with the creator’s key when signatures are already present on the transaction.

**Requirements**:  
- Update the TTv2 export logic used for hybrid signing to:  
  - Detect whether the transaction already contains one or more valid signatures.  
  - If signatures exist, do **not** add a creator signature before export.  
  - If no signatures exist, keep the current behavior of signing with the creator’s key so the transaction remains frozen for TTv1 processing.  
- Ensure that exported transactions remain valid and treated as frozen by TTv1 in both cases.  
- Verify that the hybrid fallback path (TTv2 → TTv1 → back) works correctly when the transaction already has signatures from other signers or flows.

**Acceptance criteria**:  
- When exporting a transaction that already has signatures, TTv2 does not add an extra creator signature.  
- When exporting a transaction with no signatures, TTv2 still signs with the creator’s key so TTv1 can process it safely.  
- Hybrid signing flows using these exports function correctly in TTv1 and do not require additional “dummy” signatures to act as a freeze.

items.title: Notifications not cleared when navigating between transactions or viewing new creator-signed transactions
items.body:
**Description**  
When navigating between transactions using “Next/Prev” or “Sign & next”, notifications for the transactions being viewed are not cleared, even when they should be marked as read for the current user. In addition, when a creator submits a transaction that also requires the creator’s signature, the transaction details are shown immediately and are signable. If the creator does not sign at that time but navigates back to the Transactions tab, the new transaction still appears as “new”, even though the creator already viewed its details. This makes it harder to distinguish truly new or unseen items. It looks like many, but not all, “mark as read” calls could be consolidated into a single, central place (for example, whenever the transaction details view is opened) so that any transaction the user has viewed no longer shows a “new” notification for that user.

**Steps to reproduce**  
1. Open a transaction list that shows “new” or similar notifications for transactions that require attention.  
2. Open a transaction’s details.  
3. Use “Next/Prev” or “Sign & next” to move between transactions that currently have notifications.  
4. Return to the transaction list and observe notifications for the transactions that were viewed via navigation.  
5. As a separate scenario, create a transaction that also requires the creator’s own signature.  
6. After the transaction is created and its details view is shown, do not sign it; instead, navigate back to the Transactions tab.  
7. Observe how the newly created transaction is still marked as “new” for the creator.

**Expected behavior**  
- When a transaction’s details are viewed (including when reached via “Next/Prev” or “Sign & next”), any “new” notification for that transaction should be cleared for the current user.  
- When a creator submits a transaction that they immediately view in the details screen, that transaction should no longer appear as “new” once they leave the details view, even if they did not

items.title: Observers to be used for group notifications
items.body:
Popular apps like Slack allow for another avenue for notifications. Users have mentioned that they don't check emails as frequently as they check Slack. In order to notify people as quickly as possible, there needs to be a way to add a specific email to a transaction that will receive these notifications (if I understand correctly). 

Once the observer feature is fully in place, the admin can add a Slack user that will then be added to the transactions as an observer and facilitate the Slack notification request.

items.title: 2112 transaction queries to use caches
items.body:
**Description**:
This pull request introduces significant refactoring and improvements to the transaction node querying logic, notification emission, and related testing. The most important changes include replacing legacy transaction node aggregation/filtering with a new SQL-based approach, streamlining notification updates, and updating tests and module dependencies to support these changes.

**Transaction Node Query Refactor:**

* Major refactor of `TransactionNodesService` to use direct SQL queries (via `SqlBuilderService` and `getTransactionNodesForUser`) instead of aggregating and filtering transactions in memory. This improves performance, simplifies logic, and centralizes filtering. (`back-end/apps/api/src/transactions/nodes/transaction-nodes.service.ts`) [[1]](diffhunk://#diff-f04cebaf2c191881eb159393331657994eee18f96f52e87e6eb8c043fe7db6d9L2-R18) [[2]](diffhunk://#diff-f04cebaf2c191881eb159393331657994eee18f96f52e87e6eb8c043fe7db6d9L31-L254)
* Introduction of `TRANSACTION_STATUS_COLLECTIONS` constant to standardize transaction status groupings used in node queries. (`back-end/apps/api/src/transactions/nodes/transaction-node-collections.constants.ts`)
* Addition of `SqlBuilderModule` to the transactions module dependencies to support the new SQL-based approach. (`back-end/apps/api/src/transactions/transactions.module.ts`) [[1]](diffhunk://#diff-7d803d62fab311bd9d55a16583ce0247dbcc4280dfb64e4ae7098cd15aa37737L23-R23) [[2]](diffhunk://#diff-7d803d62fab311bd9d55a16583ce0247dbcc4280dfb64e4ae7098cd15aa37737R56)

**Notification Emission Changes:**

* Refactored notification emission for transaction status updates to use a simplified payload (only `entityId`), removing additional data previously sent. This affects both `TransactionGroupsService` and `TransactionsService`. (`back-end/apps/api/src/transactions/groups/transaction-groups.service.ts`, `back-end/apps/api/src/transactions/transactions.service.ts`) [[1]](diffhunk://#diff-6d72cb8fb9bb871d20c95b9

items.title: fix: Gettransactionnodes showing irrelevant transactions
items.body:
**Summary**

  - Users were seeing transactions in their signing queue that they didn't actually need to sign. This happened because the `TransactionCachedAccount` link table didn't distinguish between sender/signer accounts and receiver accounts — so if a user's key appeared on a receiving account of a transfer, the transaction was incorrectly surfaced.
  - Added an `isReceiver` boolean column to `TransactionCachedAccount` to track whether a linked account is the receiver of the transaction.
  - Updated the `getTransactionNodesQuery` SQL to exclude receiver-only account links (`isReceiver = false` filter) in the cached-account signer branch, so transactions only appear for users who actually need to sign.
  - Terminal-status transactions (EXECUTED, FAILED, etc.) bypass the receiver filter so they remain visible in historical views.

**What changed**

  Database & Entity:
  - New migration `TransactionCachedAccountIsReceiver` adds `isReceiver` boolean column (default false) to `transaction_cached_account` table.
  - `TransactionCachedAccount` entity updated with the new column.

  Account caching (`AccountCacheService`):
  - `getAccountInfoForTransaction`, `saveAccountData`, and related methods now accept and propagate an isReceiver flag when linking transactions to cached accounts.
  - `linkTransactionToAccount` inlined (removed generic `cache.helper.ts` dependency) and sets `isReceiver` on insert.

  SQL query (`transaction.queries.ts`):
  - Cached-account signer branch now filters with `AND ta.isReceiver = false`, preventing receiver-only accounts from qualifying a user as a signer.
  - `onlyUnsigned` logic refactored from correlated `NOT EXISTS `subqueries to more efficient `LEFT JOIN ... IS NULL `pattern.
  - `buildWhereClause` simplified: `onlyUnsigned` moved into Roles, user/roles combined into a single `userContext` parameter, `addParam` exposed in return for downstream reuse.
  - Approver CTE changed from `COUNT(*) > 0` to `EXISTS` for

items.title: feat(deps): Upgrade JavaScript SDK
items.body:
Fixes [#2549 ](https://github.com/hashgraph/hedera-transaction-tool/issues/2549)

- Description:

A newer version of the JavaScript SDK includes bug fixes needed in Transaction Tool, so we should upgrade to the latest version. This upgrade may also require updating import paths if the namespace has changed from hashgraph/sdk to hiero-ledger/sdk. We should also determine whether the hashgraph/proto dependency needs to be upgraded, and if so, switch to the new namespace there as well.

- Requirements:

Upgrade Transaction Tool to the latest JS SDK version that contains the required bug fixes.
Verify whether the SDK namespace has changed from hashgraph/sdk to hiero-ledger/sdk.
Update all imports and references to match the correct namespace if the package name changed.
Determine whether hashgraph/proto also needs to be upgraded.
If the proto dependency changes, update imports and usage to the appropriate new namespace.
Confirm the application builds and runs correctly after the dependency changes.

- Acceptance criteria:

Transaction Tool uses the latest JS SDK version with the required bug fixes.
Any namespace changes are reflected consistently throughout the codebase.
Any required proto dependency updates are applied and verified.
The project compiles, tests pass, and existing functionality is not broken by the upgrade.

items.title: Notification System Fails When Transaction Requires Deleted User's Key
items.body:
**Summary**
* Fix notification failures when transactions require keys belonging to soft-deleted users
* Implement cascade soft-delete for user keys when a user is removed
* Add defensive filtering in notification and transaction services to handle edge cases

**Problem**
When a transaction required a key belonging to a soft-deleted user, the notification system would fail because:
1. NotificationReceiver entities were created for deleted users
2. TypeORM's soft-delete mechanism filtered out the user when sending emails
3. The notification process crashed due to missing user data
Solution: Hybrid Approach
This PR implements a combination of Option 1 (Filter by User Status) and Option 2 (Cascade Delete) from the [issue description](https://github.com/hashgraph/hedera-transaction-tool/issues/2016).

**What's Implemented**
1. Cascade Soft-Delete (Option 2)
When a user is deleted, all their keys are now automatically soft-deleted:
```
// users.service.ts
async removeUser(id: number): Promise<boolean> {
  // Soft-delete all user keys first
  await this.repo.manager.softDelete(UserKey, { userId: id });
  // Then soft-delete the user
  await this.repo.softRemove(user);
}
```
2. Defensive Filtering (Option 1)
Added filtering at multiple points to handle historical data and edge cases:
* receiver.service.ts: Filters deleted keys/users before building notification recipient lists
* transactions.service.ts: Validates all required signers are active before creating transactions
The filter logic: !k.deletedAt && k.user && !k.user.deletedAt
3. Transaction Creation Validation
Blocks transaction creation if any required signer is inactive, with a clear error message listing affected users.

**Pros of Hybrid Approach**
* Defense in depth: Multiple layers of protection prevent failures at different stages
* No migration required: Filtering handles historical orphaned keys without database changes
* Clear user feedback: Transaction creation fails fa

items.title: chore(deps): bump amqplib from 0.10.9 to 2.0.1 in the messaging group across 1 directory
items.body:
Bumps the messaging group with 1 update in the / directory: [amqplib](https://github.com/amqp-node/amqplib). Updates `amqplib` from 0.10.9 to 2.0.1
Release notes

Sourced from amqplib's releases.

v2.0.1

  • Remove buffer-more-ints dependency; use Node.js built-in BigInt Buffer methods (readBigInt64BE, readBigUInt64BE, writeBigInt64BE, writeBigUInt64BE) which have been available since Node.js v10.4

v2.0.0

BREAKING CHANGE

heartbeat: 0 now correctly disables heartbeats, sending 0 in the ConnectionTuneOk frame regardless of the server's suggestion. This aligns with the AMQP 0-9-1 spec, which defines heartbeat=0 as "disabled".

Previously, 0 was passed through negotiate() which treated it as "no preference", causing the server's suggested value to be used instead.

Migration: if you are passing heartbeat: 0 and want to preserve the old behaviour of accepting the server's value, omit the option or pass null instead.

Fixes #467. Supersedes #469.

1.2.0

Add bundled TypeScript type definitions (fixes #843)

For full history see CHANGELOG.md.

items.title: Notification System Fails When Transaction Requires Deleted User's Key
items.body:
**Problem**
When a transaction required a key belonging to a soft-deleted user, the notification system would fail because:

1. NotificationReceiver entities were created for deleted users
2. TypeORM's soft-delete mechanism filtered out the user when sending emails
3. The notification process crashed due to missing user data

**Solution**
1. Cascade Soft-Delete for User Keys When a user is deleted, all their keys are now automatically soft-deleted:

```
// users.service.ts
async removeUser(id: number): Promise<boolean> {
  // Soft-delete all user keys first
  await this.repo.manager.softDelete(UserKey, { userId: id });
  // Then soft-delete the user
  await this.repo.softRemove(user);
}
```

2. Notification Guards Added filtering in `receiver.service.ts` to exclude deleted keys/users before building notification recipient lists:

`const activeKeys = filterActiveUserKeys(allKeys);`

The filter logic: !k.deletedAt && k.user && !k.user.deletedAt

3. Optional userKey in TransactionSigner Updated interfaces and services to handle null/undefined userKey:
* Made userKey optional in ITransactionSignerUserKey and ITransactionSignerFull interfaces
* Added optional chaining (?.) in approvers.service.ts and observers.service.ts

**Benefits**
* Simple and focused: Only the necessary changes to fix the issue
* No migration required: Filtering handles historical orphaned keys without database changes
* Future-proof: New user deletions properly cascade to keys
* Graceful degradation: Deleted keys are filtered from notifications rather than causing crashes

items.title: feat: automation test startup to use seeded baselines
items.body:
**Description**:
This PR refactors automation startup so most suites stop paying the cost of UI registration in `beforeAll`, and it stabilizes seeded auth startup in Electron by normalizing the login route without reloading the packaged file URL.
* Add reusable non-UI local baseline seeding for local user, claims, and key pairs
* Add reusable non-UI organization baseline seeding for org users, org keys, and org session setup
* Migrate local suites, organization suites, and performance seed/setup paths off `completeRegistration()`
* Limit direct UI registration startup to registration coverage tests
* Group frontend CI automation jobs by setup profile to reduce repeated startup cost across matrix jobs
* Skip SQLite/Postgres teardown resets in CI while preserving local cleanup behavior
* Centralize startup modal dismissal in the shared app bootstrapper
* Add explicit sign-in vs registration mode detection and assertions to the login page object
* Remount the login route after baseline seeding instead of reloading the Electron file URL
* Update registration and login suites to assert the expected auth mode explicitly
* Remove the focused login suite marker

**Related issue(s)**:

Fixes #2476

**Notes for reviewer**:

- Expected reviewer smoke checks:
  - one local seeded suite, e.g. `loginTests`
  - one org seeded suite, e.g. `organizationSettingsTests`
  - `registrationTests` to confirm empty-state startup still lands in registration mode

- [x] Tested (unit, integration, etc.)

items.title: Messaging layer replacement and improvements
items.body:
**Description**:
RabbitMQ has some limitations due to our desire to maintain a lightweight backend. In order to properly set up RabbitMQ for our messaging needs, it would require a lot more resources. Nats + Jetstream will be used as the replacement. 

With this change, we now will be using a proper at-most-once queue for all messaging that should only be processed by a single replica of a single service. We will also be using a at-least-once fan out type queue for all broadcast communications to enable all connected websockets to push the proper notifications. 

Speed improvements were added as well. Transaction Group signing was a big issue. If the group was too large, and multiple keys were involved, the backend could not keep up. This causes a number of issues, including unresponsive UI, numerous emails/inapp notifications, etc. Now signing is done in a batched manner, all calls to the database are grouped were possible. 

**Related issue(s)**:

Fixes #1740 #1800 #1887 #1625 #1770 #1929 #1965 #1906

**Notes for reviewer**:
<!-- Provide logs, performance numbers or screenshots of the new functionality -->

**Checklist**

- [x] Documented (Code comments, README, etc.)
- [x] Tested (unit, integration, etc.)

items.title: refactor: split tests to optimize workers
items.body:
Description:
This PR restructures the Playwright automation suites to improve file-level parallelism and reduce shared E2E wall-clock time without changing the current sequential execution model inside each test file.

Move functional tests into suite-specific folders for local-basic, local-transactions, organization-basic, and organization-advanced
Split local transaction coverage into smaller focused files for transaction execution, drafts, workflow flows, and workflow history/details
Split advanced organization transaction coverage into focused files for status/signing, observer visibility, execution flows, and compatibility scenarios
Keep the existing shared bootstrap and fixture helpers, but apply them across the new suite layout
Increase shared frontend CI worker count from 3 to 4 to take advantage of the new file distribution
Update automation README to reflect the new suite layout, commands, and Playwright worker model
Related issue(s):
Fixes https://github.com/hashgraph/hedera-transaction-tool/issues/2599

Notes for reviewer:
The main behavioral change is scheduling. Large suites are now distributed across more files so Playwright can keep more workers busy during shared runs, while tests inside each file still execute sequentially. Coverage is being redistributed rather than fundamentally changed.

Checklist

 Documented (Code comments, README, etc.)
 Tested (unit, integration, etc.)

Release notes

Sourced from axios's releases.

v1.15.2

This release delivers prototype-pollution hardening for the Node HTTP adapter, adds an opt-in allowedSocketPaths allowlist to mitigate SSRF via Unix domain sockets, fixes a keep-alive socket memory leak, and ships supply-chain hardening across CI and security docs.

🔒 Security Fixes

  • Prototype Pollution Hardening (HTTP Adapter): Hardened the Node HTTP adapter and resolveConfig/mergeConfig/validator paths to read only own properties and use null-prototype config objects, preventing polluted auth, baseURL, socketPath, beforeRedirect, and insecureHTTPParser from influencing requests. (#10779)
  • SSRF via socketPath: Rejects non-string socketPath values and adds an opt-in allowedSocketPaths config option to restrict permitted Unix domain socket paths, returning AxiosError ERR_BAD_OPTION_VALUE on mismatch. (#10777)
  • Supply-chain Hardening: Added .npmrc with ignore-scripts=true, lockfile lint CI, non-blocking reproducible build diff, scoped CODEOWNERS, expanded SECURITY.md/THREATMODEL.md with provenance verification (npm audit signatures), 60-day resolution policy, and maintainer incident-response runbook. (#10776)

🚀 New Features

items.title: chore(deps): bump axios from 1.15.2 to 1.16.0
items.body:
Bumps [axios](https://github.com/axios/axios) from 1.15.2 to 1.16.0.
Release notes

Sourced from axios's releases.

v1.16.0 — May 2, 2026

This release adds support for the QUERY HTTP method and a new ECONNREFUSED error constant, lands a substantial wave of HTTP, fetch, and XHR adapter bug fixes around redirects, aborts, headers, and timeouts, and welcomes 23 new contributors.

⚠️ Notable Changes

A handful of fixes in this release are either security-adjacent or change observable behaviour. Please review before upgrading:

  • Fetch adapter now enforces maxBodyLength and maxContentLength. These limits were silently ignored on the fetch adapter prior to 1.16.0 — anyone relying on them as a safety net (DoS protection, accidental large uploads) had no protection. (#10795)
  • Proxy requests now preserve user-supplied Host headers. Previously, the proxy path could overwrite a custom Host. Virtual-host-style routing through a proxy will now behave correctly. (#10822)
  • Basic auth credentials embedded in URLs are now URL-decoded. If you have percent-encoded credentials in a URL (e.g. https://user:p%40ss@host), the decoded value is what now goes on the wire. (#10825)
  • parseProtocol now strictly requires a colon in the protocol separator. Strings that loosely parsed as protocols before may no longer match. (#10729)

items.title: Fixing Flickering Buttons
items.body:
**Problem**
When a user signs a transaction and it moves from one status to another, the action buttons (Sign, Cancel, etc.) in the top-right corner of the Transaction Details page flicker - they appear, disappear, and reappear inconsistently. This happens during status transitions like "Waiting for Signatures" → "Awaiting Execution".

The button flickering is caused by the fullyLoaded flag being reset to false every time the transaction data changes.

1. When you click "Sign", after signing completes, fetchTransaction() is called
2. This updates the organizationTransaction prop
3. A watcher in TransactionDetailsHeader.vue detects this change and immediately sets fullyLoaded = false
4. The visibleButtons computed property checks: if (!fullyLoaded.value) return []; - returning an empty array
5. All buttons disappear
6. The watcher then runs async operations (mirror node API call + backend API call)
7. When those complete, fullyLoaded = true is set
8. Buttons reappear

**Solution**
Buttons change from enabled/disabled state instead of being shown/hidden.

items.title: feat: support playwright attach mode
items.body:
**Description**:
This PR adds support for running Playwright against an already running Electron dev instance and for deterministically mocking native file dialogs during E2E tests. It is needed because remote debugging and native dialogs are controlled in the Electron app, not from Playwright alone.

* Add `launch` and `attach` modes to the Electron automation bootstrapper
* Connect to a running Electron app over CDP when attach mode is enabled
* Gate remote debugging and certificate-related Electron switches behind automation env vars
* Add test-only IPC and preload hooks for mocking `showOpenDialog` and `showSaveDialog`
* Replace inline dialog monkey-patching in tests with shared dialog mock helpers
* Update automation docs and example env configuration for both launch and attach flows

**Related issue(s)**:

Fixes #2472

**Notes for reviewer**:
<!-- Provide logs, performance numbers or screenshots of the new functionality -->

For attach mode, start the app from `front-end/` with:

```bash
PLAYWRIGHT_TEST=true ELECTRON_REMOTE_DEBUGGING_PORT=9222 pnpm dev
```

All Electron app-side changes are env-gated and should not affect normal user or production flows unless the automation flags are explicitly enabled.

items.title: chore(deps): bump axios from 1.15.2 to 1.16.0 in /automation
items.body:
Bumps [axios](https://github.com/axios/axios) from 1.15.2 to 1.16.0.
Release notes

Sourced from axios's releases.

v1.16.0 — May 2, 2026

This release adds support for the QUERY HTTP method and a new ECONNREFUSED error constant, lands a substantial wave of HTTP, fetch, and XHR adapter bug fixes around redirects, aborts, headers, and timeouts, and welcomes 23 new contributors.

⚠️ Notable Changes

A handful of fixes in this release are either security-adjacent or change observable behaviour. Please review before upgrading:

  • Fetch adapter now enforces maxBodyLength and maxContentLength. These limits were silently ignored on the fetch adapter prior to 1.16.0 — anyone relying on them as a safety net (DoS protection, accidental large uploads) had no protection. (#10795)
  • Proxy requests now preserve user-supplied Host headers. Previously, the proxy path could overwrite a custom Host. Virtual-host-style routing through a proxy will now behave correctly. (#10822)
  • Basic auth credentials embedded in URLs are now URL-decoded. If you have percent-encoded credentials in a URL (e.g. https://user:p%40ss@host), the decoded value is what now goes on the wire. (#10825)
  • parseProtocol now strictly requires a colon in the protocol separator. Strings that loosely parsed as protocols before may no longer match. (#10729)

items.title: fix: adjust websocket connection recovery settings and clean up code
items.body:
**Description**:
This pull request makes a few targeted updates to the WebSocket gateway in the notifications service. The main change is a reduction in the maximum disconnection duration for connection state recovery, along with additional configuration options for connection recovery.

WebSocket connection recovery configuration:

* Reduced the `maxDisconnectionDuration` for connection state recovery from 2 minutes to 30 seconds and explicitly set `skipMiddlewares` to `false` in the `@WebSocketGateway` decorator options.

Code cleanup:

* Removed commented-out legacy code for the `notifyClients` method in the `WebsocketGateway` class.

Dependency management:

* Moved the import of `ConfigService` to the top of the file for better organization.

**Related issue(s)**:

Fixes #2156 

**Checklist**

- [x] Documented (Code comments, README, etc.)
- [x] Tested (unit, integration, etc.)

items.title: fix: adjust notifications deployment replica count and improve valid …
items.body:
**Description**:
ValidStart was not being properly processed for Transaction Signer automated reminder emails. The date objects get automatically converted to strings. The processing for the email expected a date object. This is now corrected.

**Notes for reviewer**:
I reduced the notifications replica count to 1 to better reflect current production deployments.

items.title: feat: add frontend and backend tests to CI
items.body:
feat: add frontend and backend tests to CI

**Description**:
This PR expands CI coverage for the Electron/Playwright automation suites by bringing up the backend stack for organization flows and hardening the test harness for GitHub Actions reliability.
* Add backend-required organization suites to the frontend CI matrix
* Update organization test setup to reuse the payer key across users and skip unstable recovery scenarios
* Harden automation helpers with longer CI timeouts, enabled-button waits, improved selectors, localnet payer handling, and mirror node retry/debug logging
* Provision backend services in CI with Docker Compose, TLS certificates, Solo port proxies, and readiness checks
* Publish Playwright reports, backend logs, Solo diagnostics, and Kind logs for easier failure triage
* Split Docker Compose into a CI-friendly base file and a local development overlay, and document the local workflow

**Related issue(s)**:

Fixes #2301

**Notes for reviewer**:
Organization suites that require the backend now run with `backendRequired: true`.
Local backend runs use docker-compose.dev.yaml

**Checklist**

- [x] Documented (Code comments, README, etc.)
- [ ] Tested (unit, integration, etc.)

items.title: feat: automation hooks and attach-mode state handling on top of shared parallel E2E execution
items.body:
Description: This PR extracts reusable Playwright automation helpers and reorganizes shared utilities into domain-specific modules to reduce duplication and make the automation suite easier to maintain.
• Extract shared local and organization suite setup and teardown helpers
• Move repeated group, settings, and organization test actions into reusable flow helpers and fixtures
• Reorganize automation/utils into focused baselines, crypto, data, db, files, network, runtime, and setup modules
• Convert automationSupport into a thin re-export layer over the new utility modules
• Update automation pages, services, and tests to use the new module structure

Related issue(s):
Fixes #2598 

Notes for reviewer: 
This is primarily a test automation refactor with no intended functional changes. Review focus:
• extracted helpers under automation/tests/helpers
• utility moves and import rewiring under automation/utils
• suite setup/isolation changes for local and organization tests

Checklist

items.title: feat: importing signatures from TTv1 for backend storage
items.body: **Description**: Changes below implement importing from `TTv1` to `TTv2`. `Transactions` panel now displays a new `Import` button: When clicked, user is asked to choose one or more `ZIP` files generated by `TTv1`: `TTv2` reads selected `ZIP` files and displays a modal dialog which lists transactions found and indicates if they can be imported or not. items.title: fix: Ensure app is fully closed before manual install/upgrade on macOS
items.body: Fixes #2284 **PROBLEM** When a user runs a .pkg installer while the app is already open (even just minimized or sitting in the dock with no visible windows), macOS silently replaces the app bundle files under the live Electron process. On the next launch this leaves stale V8 code caches and corrupted resource references, causing the app to show the old version or a blank/broken screen. DMG installs are not affected — Finder natively blocks replacing an in-use .app. **SOLUTION** This PR adds PKG installer scripts and a single-instance lock to prevent this: - Add `preinstall` script that detects a running app, shows a native macOS dialog offering to close it (with graceful quit force-quit fallback), and silently force-quits in headless/MDM environments - Add `postinstall` script that clears stale Electron/Chromium caches (GPUCache/, Code Cache/, Cache/) for all users without touching user data - Wire scripts into the PKG build via `electron-builder.yml` - Add single-instance lock (app.requestSingleInstanceLock()) so duplicate Electron processes cannot run simultaneously - a second launch focuses the existing window instead Notes for reviewer: - The `preinstall` script handles three paths: interactive GUI (shows dialog), headless/MDM (sudo installer -pkg), and error fallback (silently force-quits) - The `postinstall` cache cleanup covers both the root app-support directory and the Partitions/persist:main/ session partition - Function definitions in `index.ts` were moved above the if/else block to avoid hoisting issues in the test environment Checklist - Documented with code comments - Tested manually

items.title: feat: update transaction group details to use cached data
items.body:
**Description**:
This pull request introduces several improvements and refactors to the transaction groups API, focusing on DTO mapping, controller flexibility, and service logic/testing. The main changes include enhanced DTO transformation and validation, support for partial/full group fetches via a new query parameter, and expanded test coverage for the service layer.

**DTO and Mapping Improvements:**

* Added a `group` property to `TransactionGroupItemDto`, with proper type transformation to `TransactionGroupDto` for improved nested DTO mapping. [[1]](diffhunk://#diff-5cb1227e322ef5e22fd91550f8a0b4ae585f13c19e40a693e5d87c1ee3aface2R3-R4) [[2]](diffhunk://#diff-5cb1227e322ef5e22fd91550f8a0b4ae585f13c19e40a693e5d87c1ee3aface2R13-R16)
* Made the `groupItem` property in `TransactionDto` optional, allowing DTOs to be constructed even when group items are omitted.
* Added comprehensive unit tests for DTO mapping, including cases for nested objects, buffer-to-hex conversion, date parsing, and validation, as well as scenarios where `groupItem` is omitted. [[1]](diffhunk://#diff-650b9957ee7aa875141eb42ed577ebd267095dca6b371c424f9ba4cb18639403R1-R55) [[2]](diffhunk://#diff-c3a2d9d0b4552ace368123371122f4821aa8e2cc2778d688191e6a2f912f7da0L31-R83)

**Controller and Service Enhancements:**

* Updated the `getTransactionGroup` endpoint to accept a `full` query parameter (parsed as boolean), enabling clients to request either a full or partial group representation. The default behavior is now explicit. [[1]](diffhunk://#diff-f7fbc1540cfb60f4c346f82c400f3d8f49294b27f6643b603d64ee424aa05138R7-R10) [[2]](diffhunk://#diff-f7fbc1540cfb60f4c346f82c400f3d8f49294b27f6643b603d64ee424aa05138R69-R71)
* Refactored the service logic to support the `full` parameter, optimizing database queries and conditional fetching of related entities (signers, approvers, observers). [[1]](diffhunk://#diff-6d72cb8fb9bb871d20c95b94f9c967c5eb286b7f9a4272204357658d4005e540L1-R13) [[2]](diffhunk:/

items.title: 1716 signatures gathered from different versions of the tool can be incorrect
items.body:
**Description**:
When viewing a transaction's details, or a transaction group's details, the app will determine if the transaction was created using the same SDK. This is done by comparing the raw bytes with the deserialized transaction's bytes. If they are not the same, the app cannot sign the transaction.

**Related issue(s)**:

Fixes #1716 

**Notes for reviewer**:
The front-end needed a bunch of reformatting to follow the projects format rules.

**Checklist**

- [x] Documented (Code comments, README, etc.)
- [x] Tested (unit, integration, etc.)

items.title: fix: bypass the need for the transaction parameter.
items.body:
**Description**:
When importing from TTv1, the entire signatureMap is serialized. Now, the signatureMap can be deserialized and sent directly to the backend, it does not need to first be added to the transaction. 

Either solution would work, but this approach allows the developer more control over which signatures will be uploaded instead of having to manipulate the transaction directly, in some cases. 

**Checklist**

- [x] Documented (Code comments, README, etc.)
- [x] Tested (unit, integration, etc.)

items.title: feat: add frontend upgrade prompts based on version check response and failed guard
items.body:
**Description**:
Front end changes related to the version check and failing version check guard
New update modals are introduced:
* for mandatory updated when the current version is below the `minimumSupportedVersion` and when the guard is failing
* an optional one when `currentVersion >= minimumSupportedVersion` and `currentVersion < latestSupportedVersion`

The current flow is the following:
```
┌─────────────────┐
│   App Start     │
│   (Login Page)  │
└────────┬────────┘
         │
         ▼
┌─────────────────────────┐
│  User enters credentials│
│  Clicks Login           │
└────────┬────────────────┘
         │
         ▼
┌─────────────────────────┐
│  POST /auth/login       │
│  (goes through guard)   │
└────────┬────────────────┘
         │
    ┌────┴────────────────┐
    │                     │
    ▼                     ▼
Guard passes          Guard rejects
(version >= min)      (version < min)
    │                     │
    ▼                     ▼
Login succeeds        426 + updateUrl
    │                     │
    ▼                     ▼
Call version-check    Mandatory modal
    │                 (blocking, can't login)
    │
    ├── updateAvailable → Optional modal
    │
    └── current → No modal, continue
    
```

**Related issue(s)**:

Fixes https://github.com/hashgraph/hedera-transaction-tool/issues/2104

**Notes for reviewer**:
This PR includes changed from https://github.com/hashgraph/hedera-transaction-tool/pull/2129 
Should be rebased and merged after the other one.

**Checklist**

- [x] Documented (Code comments, README, etc.)
- [x] Tested (unit, integration, etc.)

**Description**:

Backend Updates:
Added the additionalData property to the Notification entity, emitted during the creation of a transaction.

Frontend Implementation:

items.title: Export Transaction as .txsig
items.body:
**Description**:
Patch 0.9.1 altered how exporting worked. The exported transaction will now have the extension of .txsig. 

This was done to ensure other work flows that use these exported files don't need to change how they work. Also, the concept behind .tx and .txsig was that .tx only contains the transaction, where as .txsig also contains the signatures. All exported transactions from this app will contain signatures, if applicable.

items.title: Verify transaction group valid_start override behavior
items.body:
**Description**:  
Observed transaction group validStart value overriding individual transaction validStart values. Unable to reproduce initially, but observed again days later. Confirm this is not a regression vs user error (wrong branch/environment) and ensure group settings never override individual transaction validStart values unless the transaction validStart is < group validStart.

**Investigation steps:**
- Review recent code changes affecting validStart handling
- Document exact reproduction steps when observed

**Acceptance criteria:**
- Group validStart never overrides individual transaction validStart unless group validStart > transaction validStart
- Clear reproduction steps documented (or confirmed as user error)

items.title: v0.27.0 Final release task
items.body:
# Release workflow

## Final Release

### Pre-requisites
- [x] Beta `v<major.minor.patch>-beta.<number>` deployed and stable
- [x] Manual testing issue created from `docs/test-scenarios.md`
- [x] Manual testing completed and beta approved

### Final versioning
- [x] Update all `package.json` versions to `<major.minor.patch>`
- [x] Run `pnpm i` in `backend/`, `frontend/`, and `automation/`
- [x] Commit and open PR with version, lockfile, NOTICE, and other relevant changes
- [x] Get PR approval and merge

### Apply core release steps for final
- [x] Build frontend artifacts
- [x] Notarize frontend artifacts (all .pkg and .dmg files)
- [x] Build and push backend images with correct tags

### Final tagging and release
- [x] Create final git tag: `v<major.minor.patch>`
- [x] Create GitHub release for `v<major.minor.patch>` with final release notes
- [x] Create a new branch on DevOps-GitOps:
  - [x] Update `development` overlays to use this version
  - [x] Update `finance` overlays to use this version (if applicable)
  - [x] Update `devops` overlays to use this version (if applicable)

items.title: Notifications still not reliably cleared after signing from “Ready to sign” tab
items.body:
**Description**  
This is a follow‑up to issue https://github.com/hashgraph/hedera-transaction-tool/issues/2319. The original fix improved notification clearing when signing from the “Ready to sign” tab, but it does not fully address the problem. In particular, if the user interacts quickly with the UI (for example, signing and immediately navigating) or if notification refreshes occur while the signing operation is in progress, the “new transaction ready to sign” notification is not always cleared. This results in transactions remaining flagged as “new” or “ready to sign” even after the user has successfully signed and there is no further action required from them.

**Steps to reproduce**  
1. Create multiple multisig transactions that will appear in the “Ready to sign” tab in status “waiting for signatures”.  
2. Open the “Ready to sign” tab and ensure that “new transaction ready to sign” notifications are visible for these transactions.  
3. Attempt to sign all of the transactions quickly in succession from the “Ready to sign” tab.  
4. Observe the incorrect badge number indicating new transactions on the “Ready to sign” tab.

**Expected behavior**  
- After the user signs each transaction from the “Ready to sign” tab, the “new transaction ready to sign” notification for that transaction should reliably be cleared for that user, even when signing many transactions quickly.  
- Notification state should accurately reflect whether the user has any remaining signing actions for each transaction.

**Actual behavior**  
- When signing multiple transactions quickly from the “Ready to sign” tab, some “new transaction ready to sign” notifications are not cleared.  
- The user continues to see stale notifications for transactions they have already signed and cannot act on, which is confusing and makes it harder to identify truly new items that require attention.

items.title: Generate a report that outlines signing and version trends for organizations
items.body:
### Problem

Create a backend-generated report that shows, per user, the percentage of transactions they have signed out of all transactions that required their signature, along with the frontend application version currently installed by the user. This report would represent the users in one organization. The time period of the report should be configurable. 

This report will help with adoption analysis, troubleshooting signing issues, and correlating behavior with frontend versions.


### Solution

The report should include:
- Signature completion percentage (`(signedTransactions / requiredTransactions) * 100`)
- How long from when transaction was created to when the user signed (response time)
- Frontend application version

Output Format: CSV

### Alternatives

_No response_

items.title: Ready to Sign loading issues
items.body:
The Ready To Sign tab does not load as quickly as it should. This is experienced when there are many transactions that are in progress (Waiting for Signatures or Waiting for Execution). 

The reason for the slow loading is that every time the front end makes a request for all transactions that the user needs to sign, the back end needs to do a lot of computing:
1. Pull each transaction that is either Waiting for Signatures or Waiting for Execution
2. Determine if the user has already signed the transaction (via transaction_signer table and also checking the signatures in the transaction bytes).
3. Determine which accounts are involved and need to sign
4. Look up the current keys for those accounts
5. Determine if the user is an owner of any of the public keys obtained from mirror node
6. Return all transactions the user needs to sign.

Because of all these required steps, the user experience is poor. The results take too long to compute.

items.title: Transaction Group needs an atomic option
items.body:
Transaction group needs an option to make the group atomic. This would require that all transactions are submitted together, or none will be submitted. This will not ensure that all transactions will reach consensus. It is a pre-check only. 

The check would require that all transactions that are not canceled have all the proper signatures before any of them are submitted.

items.title: Investigate impact of cancelation on large group signing
items.body:
**Description**:  
During the creation and signing of a large transaction group, the creator canceled the first transaction in the group while another user was signing the group, and the signing user reported that the signing seemed to complete without clear indication of success, while the backend only recorded 95 of 148 transactions as signed. We must determine whether canceling the transaction directly caused this inconsistent signing state, or whether it was due to performance degradation or client refreshing triggered by the cancelation, and ensure that cancelation does not interfere with signing events in large groups going forward.

**Reported behavior:**
- Transaction group size: 148 transactions.  
- First transaction in the group was canceled by the creator during the signing process of another user.  
- Signing user experience:
  - Reported that signing “completed” but without clear success indication.  
- Backend state:
  - Only 95 of 148 transactions recorded as signed.  

**Investigation goals:**
- Determine whether:
  - The cancelation of the first transaction in the group directly interrupted or invalidated signing events.  
  - Performance issues (e.g., long-running operations, high latency, or UI refreshes) caused partial signing or lost events.  
  - Any race conditions exist between:
    - Group signing requests.  
    - Transaction cancelation requests (single or group-level).  
- Identify whether:
  - The signing UI can show “complete” while some signing requests fail or time out.  
  - The client stops sending signing requests after a cancelation-related state change or refresh.  
  - The backend processes signing operations in a way that can leave the group partially signed without surfacing errors to the client.  

**Required analysis steps:**
- Reproduce similar conditions in a test environment:
  - Create a large transaction group (≥148 transactions).  
  - Start a group signing flow as one user.  
  - Cancel:
    - The first transaction i

items.title: Clear notifications on sign using API response, not websocket
items.body:
**Description**:  
After signing a transaction, if the transaction is marked "unread", the backend currently marks it as read and sends a "delete notification" message over the websocket. Multiple users report that their notifications are not being removed when they sign. We should not rely on a websocket push to clear notifications; the UI should clear notifications based on the API response from the sign action so it is more responsive and less brittle.

**Requirements**:  
- Update the sign-transaction flow so that the API response explicitly indicates that the related notification should be cleared (e.g., include a flag or updated notification list/state).  
- Update the frontend to:
  - Clear the notification immediately based on the successful sign API response, where the response includes the "delete notification" message.  
  - Remove the websocket "delete notification" message after signing.  
- Add logging/telemetry around notification clearing to help confirm behavior in production.

**Acceptance criteria**:  
- After successfully signing a transaction, the related notification is cleared immediately in the UI using only the API response.  
- Users no longer observe stale notifications remaining after sign.

items.title: Regression: TTv1 signature import modal no longer appears and imports do not start
items.body:
**Description**  
A regression was introduced in the TTv1 signature import flow.

**Current behavior**  
- After selecting one or more TTv1 signature zip files to import, the import modal no longer appears.  
- No import actually starts and there is no feedback to the user.

**Expected behavior**  
- After selecting valid TTv1 signature zip file(s), the import confirmation/progress modal should appear.  
- The selected zip file(s) should be queued and imported as before.  
- Multi-zip selection should be supported so users can import several TTv1 signature archives in one operation.

**Steps to reproduce**  
1. Go to the TTv1 signatures import screen.  
2. Click "Import Signatures from File" to open the file picker.  
3. Select one or more TTv1 signature `.zip` files.  
4. Confirm the selection.

**Actual result**  
- The dialog closes but no modal appears.  
- No progress or error is shown, and no signatures are imported.

**Additional notes**  
- The zip selection should be multi selection enabled.

items.title: Simplify email content and remove detailed transaction data
items.body:
**Description**:  
Recent feedback on the new email design suggests that listing individual transactions (especially in large numbers) is not very helpful, and including detailed data in emails may introduce security and privacy risks. Update notification emails to be simpler and more high-level, describing the type and meaning of the notification without embedding transaction details.

**Requirements**:  
- Review all notification email templates that currently include transaction lists or detailed transaction data.  
- Simplify email bodies to:
  - Describe the event type and what it means (e.g., “You have transactions ready to execute”, “Some transactions have expired”).  
  - Avoid listing individual transaction IDs, amounts, or other sensitive details.  
  - Encourage users to open the app to see full details (e.g., “View details in the Hedera Transaction Tool”).

items.title: Emails failed to send on a production environment
items.body:
## Notification System Fails When Transaction Requires Deleted User's Key

### Description
When a transaction is created that requires a key belonging to a soft-deleted user, the notification system creates a `NotificationReceiver` entity but fails when attempting to send the email notification. This occurs because TypeORM filters out soft-deleted users, causing the notification process to fail.

### Steps to Reproduce
1. User A has a key assigned to them
2. User A is deleted (soft delete)
3. Create a transaction that requires User A's key
4. Share the transaction to the backend
5. Backend creates `NotificationReceiver` entity for the deleted user
6. System attempts to send email notification
7. **Error:** TypeORM doesn't return the soft-deleted user
8. Notification system fails

### Root Cause
The notification system identifies required signers based on keys without checking if the key owner has been deleted. TypeORM's soft delete mechanism then prevents the user from being retrieved during the email sending process, causing a failure.

### Proposed Solutions

#### Option 1: Filter Keys by User Status
When creating notifications based on required keys, verify that each key belongs to a non-deleted user before creating the `NotificationReceiver` entity.

**Pros:**
- Maintains existing key data
- Minimal database changes
- Clear separation of concerns

**Cons:**
- Requires additional query or join during notification creation
- Deleted user's keys remain in the system

#### Option 2: Cascade Delete Keys
When a user is deleted, also delete (or soft-delete) all keys associated with that user.

**Pros:**
- Cleaner data model
- Prevents orphaned keys
- Simpler notification logic

**Cons:**
- May impact audit trail if keys need to be retained for historical records
- Requires migration strategy for existing deleted users

### Implementation Considerations
- [ ] Decide on solution approach (Option 1, 2, or hybrid)
- [ ] Determine if soft-deleted user keys should be retaine

items.title: Show client version and update availability for users in contact list
items.body:
Admin feedback: The old tool would output a file indicating when a user had upgraded to a newer version of the tool. This functionality is still desired for the new tool.

**Description**  
Admins would like visibility into which client versions users are running so they can identify who needs to update. When a user logs into an org, the client version is already tracked and stored by the backend. The contact list and user details views should surface this information and indicate when an update is available.

**Requirements**

- Enhance the contact list so that:  
  - Each user entry shows an “Update available” indicator if any of that user’s registered clients are on a version that can be upgraded based on the org’s current minimum/target version logic.  
  - Users with no available updates continue to display as they do today (no extra indicator).

- Enhance the user details view so that:  
  - It lists all clients registered for that user (e.g., by device name, platform, or client ID, depending on what the backend provides).  
  - For each client, display the last known client version that registered with the backend.  
  - If applicable, also show whether an update is available for each client (e.g., via a badge or status text next to the version).

- General behavior:  
  - Version and “update available” status should refresh based on the latest data sent from clients on login or periodic check‑ins.  
  - The feature should work across orgs, respecting any org‑specific version policies (minimum required, recommended, etc.).  

**Acceptance Criteria**

- Admins can see an “Update available” indicator in the contact list for any user with at least one outdated client.  
- Selecting a user from the contact list shows a details page listing all known clients and their last registered versions.  
- For a test setup with mixed client versions (some current, some outdated), the indicators and version information are accurate and consistent with the backend data.

items.title: Electron app freezes with "Cannot set threshold key as file key" when lowering threshold on File Update transaction
items.body:
### Description

Description:

When creating a File Update transaction with a complex key containing multiple keys, changing the threshold from 2 of 2 to 1 of 2 throws an unhandled Error: Cannot set threshold key as file key from the Hiero SDK. The error is not caught by the UI, no user-facing error message is shown, and the Electron app becomes frozen / unresponsive.

Hedera's File service does not accept a threshold KeyList as a file key — file keys must be a plain KeyList where all keys are required to sign. The app currently allows the user to configure such a key, then crashes at transaction build time.

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/5418db85-65da-44ed-9ac6-7efe6398109c

### Steps to reproduce

Steps to reproduce:
1. Create a File Update transaction.
2. Set a valid File ID.
3. In the key selector, add a complex key with two child keys and threshold 2 of 2.
4. Lower the threshold from 2 to 1 (so it becomes 1 of 2).
5. Observe the terminal / DevTools console and the UI.

Expected behavior:
- The UI either prevents configuring a threshold-based KeyList for file keys (with a clear inline validation message explaining that file keys must require all signatures), or
- Catches the SDK error and surfaces a user-friendly toast/error message without freezing the app.

Actual behavior:
- Unhandled promise rejection is thrown from setKeys in the Hiero SDK.
- Electron window freezes and requires restart.

Error logs:
Renderer window:

> [renderer.window] Unhandled promise rejection {
>   reason: {
>     message: 'Cannot set threshold key as file key',
>     name: 'Error',
>     ...
>    }
> }

DevTools console:

> Uncaught (in promise) Error: Cannot set threshold key as file key
>     at setFileInfo (createTransactions.ts:327:17)
>     at createFileUpdateTransaction (createTransactions.ts:363:3)
>     at Proxy.<anonymous> (FileUpdate.vue:39:5)
>     at ComputedRefImpl.fn (BaseTransaction.vue:115:36)
>     at Proxy._sfc_render (BaseTransaction.vue:355:43)

items.title: Notifications service errors when creator key has been deleted
items.body:
**Description**  
When the notifications service attempts to calculate which users are affected by a transaction status change, it fails if the creator key has been deleted. In this scenario, the service tries to look up the user ID associated with the deleted creator key and throws an error. Since this logic is only used to notify users, a missing/deleted creator key should not cause the process to fail.

**Expected behavior**  
- When the creator key for a transaction has been deleted, the notifications service should skip that user and continue processing other recipients.  
- No errors should be thrown solely due to a deleted creator key during the “affected users” calculation.

**Actual behavior**  
- The notifications service attempts to resolve the user ID for the deleted creator key.  
- This lookup fails and results in an error, interrupting the notification flow.

**Steps to reproduce**  
1. Create a transaction with a given creator key.  
2. Delete that creator key from the system.  
3. Trigger a status change on the transaction that would normally send notifications.  
4. Observe that the notifications service throws an error when resolving the creator’s user ID.

**Proposed solution**  
- Update the notifications service logic so that, when it cannot resolve a user ID for the creator key (because the key is deleted or missing), it logs the situation and excludes that user from the notification recipients instead of throwing an error.

items.title: Signing Activity Reporting - MVP
items.body:
## Summary
In V2, Hashgraph staff currently have no automated way to query signing participation across transactions.  This MVP introduces four read-only API endpoints that return signing activity data as downloadable CSV files — queryable by date range + account, by specific transaction ID, by group transaction ID, and by individual signer.
 
## Description
This MVP covers four API endpoints that output data in CSV.  App or Admin UI changes are out of scope.
 
---
 
## Endpoint Descriptions
 NOTE:  Specific endpoint structure and naming suggested but not required.

### 1. Query by Date Range + Account
 
`GET /reports/signing/by-account`
 
| Parameter | Type | Required | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| `accountId` | string | yes | The Hedera account ID to scope the query (e.g. `0.0.55`) |
| `startDate` | ISO 8601 date | yes | Start of the date range (inclusive) |
| `endDate` | ISO 8601 date | no | End of the date range (inclusive). Defaults to today if omitted. |
 
**Suggested filename:** `signing-report-account-{accountId}-{startDate}-{endDate}.csv`
 
---
 
### 2. Query by Transaction ID
 
`GET /reports/signing/by-transaction`
 
| Parameter | Type | Required | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| `transactionId` | string | yes | The internal transaction ID to query |
 
**Suggested filename:** `signing-report-transaction-{transactionId}.csv`
 
---
 
### 3. Query by Group Transaction ID
 
`GET /reports/signing/by-group`
 
The work for this endpoint includes exposing the (TT) group ID as a queryable parameter and including both the group ID and the individual transaction data in the CSV output.
 
| Parameter | Type | Required | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| `groupId` | string | yes | The ID of the group transaction to query |
 
**Suggested filename:** `signing-report-group-{groupId}.csv`
 
---
 
### 4. Query by User (Signer)
 
`GET /reports/signing/by-email`
 
| Parameter | Type | Required | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| `email` | string (email) | yes | The re

items.title: Do not broadcast cache refresh notifications when no data changed
items.body:
**Description**  
The mirror node currently refreshes several caches on a fixed schedule (e.g., every N seconds/minutes). After each scheduled refresh it sends a “refresh” notification to all connected users/clients, instructing them to reload their data, even when the underlying cache data has not changed since the previous refresh. This causes unnecessary client updates and wasted network usage.

We need to ensure that refresh notifications are only sent when the cache content actually changes.

**Acceptance Criteria**

- When a scheduled cache refresh runs and the cache data remains identical to its previous state:
  - No refresh notification is emitted to users/clients.
- When a scheduled cache refresh detects any change in the cached data (new items, removed items, or updated values):
  - A refresh notification is emitted to all relevant connected users/clients.

items.title: Implement Modular Automation Setup Services
items.body:
## Summary

Refactor the current automation utilities by introducing modular services responsible for application bootstrap and transaction environment setup. This change aims to improve maintainability, readability, and reuse across automation tests.

## Problem

The existing automation utility module is monolithic and mixes multiple responsibilities such as:

* App lifecycle management
* Transaction environment configuration
* Key imports
* Localnet payer provisioning
* Environment and network constants

This structure makes the code harder to maintain, extend, and reuse across tests.

## Proposed Solution

Split the automation utility into focused services and constants.

Key changes:

* Extract application launch, reset, and shutdown logic into **AppBootstrapper**
* Separate transaction environment setup into dedicated services:
  * configuration service
  * key import service
  * localnet payer provisioning service
* Replace `util.ts` with `automationSupport.ts`
* Centralize environment variables and network constants
* Update automation pages and tests to use the new exports
* Improve retry and polling behavior around transaction and mirror node flows

## Tasks

* [ ] Implement `AppBootstrapper` service
* [ ] Create services for transaction environment setup
* [ ] Replace `util.ts` with `automationSupport.ts`
* [ ] Move environment and network constants to a centralized module
* [ ] Update automation pages to use the new services
* [ ] Update tests to use the new exports
* [ ] Adjust retry and polling defaults for transaction and mirror node flows

## Acceptance Criteria

* Automation setup logic is modularized into dedicated services
* Existing tests pass without

items.title: Investigate receiver_queue_worker NATS consumer timeouts and unprocessed notification messages
items.body:
**Description**  
The NATS consumer `receiver_queue_worker` is failing to process messages for at least the subjects `notifications.queue.transaction.status-update` and `notifications.queue.transaction.update`. After attempting to process the current notifications, the consumer times out and is removed. In contrast, the `email_queue_worker` never times out and continues to wait for messages that should be produced by `receiver_queue_worker`, causing the notification flow to stall.

We need to diagnose why `receiver_queue_worker` is not successfully processing or acknowledging these messages, and why the consumer is timing out and being deleted, then fix the underlying issues so that messages on these subjects are handled reliably.

**Acceptance Criteria**

- Root cause is identified for:
  - Why `receiver_queue_worker` is failing to process messages on `notifications.queue.transaction.status-update` and `notifications.queue.transaction.update` (e.g., deserialization errors, handler exceptions, ack logic, subscription configuration, or subject mismatch).
  - Why the NATS consumer for `receiver_queue_worker` is timing out and being removed instead of remaining healthy or recovering.
- `receiver_queue_worker`:
  - Reliably consumes and processes messages from the affected subjects under normal load.
  - Acknowledges messages correctly (or negatively acknowledges them with appropriate retries) so they are not stuck or repeatedly causing timeouts.
  - Logs clear errors when a message cannot be processed, without causing the consumer to be removed.
- The NATS consumer configuration (timeouts, max delivery, idle heartbeat, etc., if applicable) is reviewed and adjusted as needed so the worker does not time out and get deleted under expected operating conditions.
- `email_queue_worker` correctly receives messages or signals from `receiver_queue_worker` once processing succeeds, and no longer waits indefinitely due to upstream failures.
- Add or update automated tests or inte

items.title: Uploading signatures creates too many transaction_signer entities
items.body:
When a user signs a transaction, the signature gets uploaded to the backend. The signature is actually multiple signatures, one for each node in the list of nodes in the transaction. 

The signature bytes are not stored in the database inside the transaction_signer table. It is stored in the transaction itself. For this reason, there is no need for multiple transaction_signer entities.

Be sure that uploading signatures only saves a single transaction_signer entity per userKey per transaction.

items.title: Refactor Backend Notification System
items.body:
The notification system, as implemented, on the backend is not actually scalable. Currently, all messaging sent from one service to another is fire-and-forget, and is only processed by a single replica. This means that when api sends anything to be processed by chain, RabbitMQ only allows 1 chain service to get the message and process it. Same thing with the notification service.

**Issues with design**
With the current design, there is no acknowledgement that the consumer of the message actually consumes the message. In addition, in app notifications need to be fanned out to all connected users. It works for now due to all backend deployments using a single notification replica. If more are provisioned, it will no longer work.

**Solution**
All consumers should acknowledge, always. Whether manually acknowledged (for EventPattern where RabbitMQ needs to know), or automatically (for MessagePattern where the producer is waiting), we don't want any messages to get lost.  
Email and InApp notifications will need to be separated into different queues. Email can be a simple queue, like it currently is. But InApp notifications needs to be a queue coupled with a fanout exchange. This means that a lot of the notification service code will need to be separated out, and the notification logic will need to be redesigned to handle these notifications separately.

items.title: Manual 'Sign Transaction' reminder notification does not clear from notification popup
items.body: I created a transaction, then shared it with the org. I then manually clicked 'Remind Signers'. Now that signer has a notification in the notification popup that won't go away even when clicked. The database shows it to be like any other notification, but isRead is false, and they type is 'TRANSACTION_WAITING_FOR_SIGNATURES_REMINDER_MANUAL'. The notification should appear the same as if it were the original notification. It should replace the original notification, if it still exists, so both notifications don't show up in the app. Once the notification is clicked (or the transaction is viewed) the notification should be removed, just like the original notification sent to signers.

items.title: TTv2 importing from TTv2
items.body:
TTv2 needs to be able to import transactions after exporting. This will allow for an offline solution, where the user can export transactions and manually share them with others. 

Importing of transactions should work for single and group transactions. When either type of transaction is imported, it will need to ensure that all transaction properties are retained. Any signatures on the imported transaction will also need to be retained. 

This importing feature needs to work with a 'new' transaction, as well as with 'duplicate' transactions. In the case of a duplicate transaction, where the app already has a copy of the transaction, the signatures need to be collated and saved to the stored transaction.

User Flow:
1. User A creates a transaction that requires multiple signers in the TTv2 application
2. Users in the organization can sign the transaction 
3. User A exports the transaction to a User B who is not part of the organization
4. User A sends the transaction to User B
5. User B imports the transaction into another TTv2 app
6. User B signs the transaction
7. User B manually exports the signed transaction and sends the signed transaction back to User A
8. User B imports the signed transaction

Signatures are on the transaction when we convert them into bytes.

items.title: Improve cache refresh error handling for offline mirror nodes
items.body:
**Description:**  
Cache refreshing doesn't handle offline/unavailable mirror nodes gracefully. When a mirror node goes down, the current error handling blocks processing and produces verbose/less-useful logs. The system should treat mirror node failures as non-blocking, log them succinctly, and potentially implement backoff per offending network.  

**Current behavior:**  
```
Mirror node X offline → Cache refresh blocks → Processing stalls → Verbose error spam in logs
```

**Expected behavior:**  
```
Mirror node X offline → Non-blocking error → Succinct log entry → Continue processing other nodes
→ Optional: Backoff retry for network X specifically
```

**Scope:**  
- Make mirror node failures **non-blocking** (continue processing other nodes)  
- Improve error logging to be more succinct/actionable  
- Track failures per network/mirror node for backoff logic  
- Ensure cache refresh failures don't halt transaction processing  

**Acceptance criteria:**  
- [ ] Mirror node timeout/offline doesn't block cache refresh for other nodes  
- [ ] Cache refresh failures are logged succinctly (network + node + error type)  
- [ ] Processing continues immediately despite individual node failures  
- [ ] Per-network backoff tracking prevents log spam from repeated failures  
- [ ] Notification/messaging processing is never blocked by cache refresh errors  

**Nice to have:**  
- Circuit breaker per mirror node/network    
- Metrics for mirror node health/failure rates

items.title: Notify users on cache table updates via websocket
items.body:
## Description

When the cron job updates an account or node in the `cached_account` or `cached_node` tables, send a websocket notification to all connected users via `notifyClients()`. This triggers a client-side data refresh to reflect the latest cached data.

## Requirements

- In the cron job refresh logic, after successfully updating a record in:
  - `cached_account`
  - `cached_node`
- Leverage the notification system to broadcast a notification to all connected users (`notifyClients()`). 
- Notification payload should include:
  - userIds of all users that own the keys belonging to the updated entities (notifyClients() does not current make full use of these userIds, but will in the future).
- Clients receiving the notification refresh their local transaction lists or relevant UI data.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Cron job updates a cached account/node → websocket notification sent via `notifyClients()`.
- All connected users receive the notification with correct entity details.
- Clients handle the notification by refreshing relevant data (transaction lists, etc.).
- No notification sent on failed updates or `lastCheckedAt` updates without data changes.
- Tests verify notification is triggered only on successful data updates in cache tables.

items.title: Sign button doesn't disappear after signing
items.body:
### Problem

When signing a transaction, the sign button doesn't go away even though I got a toast message saying the transaction was signed successfully. This is caused by a connection issue with the WebSocketGateway (e.g. ERROR [WebsocketGateway] Socket client connected without user). 
As this bug allowed for a transition to be signed multiple times, it was noted that each time the transaction was signed, a new transaction was essentially created and submitted to the network. This is likely due to the transaction submitting to the network between the first signature and the second.
Due to multiple signings/transactions, the first transaction was successful, but subsequent transactions were marked as duplicate.

### Solution

The root cause of the issue needs to be addressed, as well as adding safeguards for potentially related issues.
1. Discover why the websocket did not reconnect. This specific case happened due to a user having the app open and running while the notification service pod was redeployed. Websocket should have reconnected, but doesn't appear to have done so properly.
2. Determine if a client side health check and reconnect to the server feature is possible
3. Sign button could be changed to not rely solely on the websocket notification, but also on the response to the signing of the transaction. If a transaction is signed and the server responds with a successful upload, the sign button can be hidden.
4. On the back-end, if the transaction is already being executed, prevent further signatures
5. Ensure that a transaction doesn't get 'duplicated' due to the above.
6. When duplicate transactions are found in history, either display all transactions, or at least display the transaction that is not a duplicate so that history will show that the first transaction was successful. 
7. If a transaction isn't actually duplicated, but instead has its status/response code updated due to the duplicate submission, don't allow the duplicate submission
