items.title: fix: Improve cache refresh error handling for offline mirror nodes
items.body:
Fixes #2285

**Problem**
When a mirror node goes offline or becomes unavailable, the cache refresh mechanism blocks processing entirely. A single failing mirror node network stalls refresh operations for all networks, producing verbose and repetitive error logs that obscure actionable information. This means transaction processing can be delayed by infrastructure issues outside the system's control.

**Solution**
Implements a per-network circuit breaker pattern (`MirrorNodeCircuitBreaker` service) that isolates mirror node failures by network, preventing a single unhealthy network from blocking cache refresh for healthy ones.

Key Changes:
- Circuit breaker with three states (`CLOSED` / `OPEN` / `HALF_OPEN`): Tracks consecutive failures per mirror node network. After a configurable failure threshold (default: 3), the circuit opens and skips that network's refresh attempts entirely until a reset timeout elapses (default: 60s), at which point it transitions to half-open and probes with a limited number of attempts before fully recovering.
- Non-blocking, per-network processing: `refreshStaleAccounts() `and `refreshStaleNodes()` now group entities by `mirrorNetwork` and process each group independently. If one network's circuit is open, its entities are skipped while all other networks continue refreshing normally.
- Concurrency guard: A `refreshInProgress` flag prevents overlapping cache refresh executions, avoiding redundant work during slow refreshes.
- Aggregated error logging: Per-entity errors are collected into a `Set` per network and logged once as a single aggregate warning (e.g., `Account refresh failures for network "testnet": timeout; connection refused`), replacing repetitive per-entity error spam. 
- Configurable via environment variables:
   - `CIRCUIT_BREAKER_FAILURE_THRESHOLD` (default: 3)
   - `CIRCUIT_BREAKER_RESET_TIMEOUT_MS` (default: 60000)
   - `CIRCUIT_BREAKER_HALF_OPEN_MAX_ATTEMPTS` (default: 1)

---

**Before:**
Mirror nod

items.title: 2112 transaction queries to use caches
items.body:
**Description**:
This pull request introduces significant refactoring and improvements to the transaction node querying logic, notification emission, and related testing. The most important changes include replacing legacy transaction node aggregation/filtering with a new SQL-based approach, streamlining notification updates, and updating tests and module dependencies to support these changes.

**Transaction Node Query Refactor:**

* Major refactor of `TransactionNodesService` to use direct SQL queries (via `SqlBuilderService` and `getTransactionNodesForUser`) instead of aggregating and filtering transactions in memory. This improves performance, simplifies logic, and centralizes filtering. (`back-end/apps/api/src/transactions/nodes/transaction-nodes.service.ts`) [[1]](diffhunk://#diff-f04cebaf2c191881eb159393331657994eee18f96f52e87e6eb8c043fe7db6d9L2-R18) [[2]](diffhunk://#diff-f04cebaf2c191881eb159393331657994eee18f96f52e87e6eb8c043fe7db6d9L31-L254)
* Introduction of `TRANSACTION_STATUS_COLLECTIONS` constant to standardize transaction status groupings used in node queries. (`back-end/apps/api/src/transactions/nodes/transaction-node-collections.constants.ts`)
* Addition of `SqlBuilderModule` to the transactions module dependencies to support the new SQL-based approach. (`back-end/apps/api/src/transactions/transactions.module.ts`) [[1]](diffhunk://#diff-7d803d62fab311bd9d55a16583ce0247dbcc4280dfb64e4ae7098cd15aa37737L23-R23) [[2]](diffhunk://#diff-7d803d62fab311bd9d55a16583ce0247dbcc4280dfb64e4ae7098cd15aa37737R56)

**Notification Emission Changes:**

* Refactored notification emission for transaction status updates to use a simplified payload (only `entityId`), removing additional data previously sent. This affects both `TransactionGroupsService` and `TransactionsService`. (`back-end/apps/api/src/transactions/groups/transaction-groups.service.ts`, `back-end/apps/api/src/transactions/transactions.service.ts`) [[1]](diffhunk://#diff-6d72cb8fb9bb871d20c95b9

items.title: Showing the freeze type on the transaction list table
items.body: **Problem** To help move faster during releases and in the narrow time window for abort transactions, the Transaction `Freeze` type needs to be added in the` Transaction Type` column of the transaction list table. **Solution** The backend does not store the specific freeze transaction type (Freeze Only, Prepare Upgrade, Freeze Upgrade, Freeze Abort) - it only stores the generic "FREEZE" type in the database. The freeze subtype information is only available within the serialized transactionBytes. To minimize the impact on the codebase and avoid backend changes, a **frontend-only approach** was implemented: 1. New utility function `getFreezeTypeForTransaction() `in **transactions.ts**: * When the frontend detects a freeze transaction, it makes an additional API call to `getTransactionById()` to fetch the full transaction details including transactionBytes * Deserializes the transaction bytes using the Hedera SDK * Extracts the `freezeType` code from the FreezeTransaction instance * Results are cached in a Map to avoid redundant API calls for the same transaction 2. New utility function `getDisplayTransactionType() `in **transactions.ts**: * Accepts either an SDK Transaction or Uint8Array * For freeze transactions, extracts and returns the specific freeze type string * Falls back to standard transaction type for non-freeze transactions 3. Updated components: * **TransactionNodeRow.vue** (Ready to Sign, In Progress, Ready for Execution, History tabs in organization mode): Added a watcher that detects freeze transactions and fetches the freeze type on-demand * **History.vue** (personal mode): Uses the existing body field from local transactions to extract freeze type * **Drafts.vue**: Parses the transactionBytes field to display specific freeze types for draft transactions **Before**

items.title: Messaging layer replacement and improvements
items.body:
**Description**:
RabbitMQ has some limitations due to our desire to maintain a lightweight backend. In order to properly set up RabbitMQ for our messaging needs, it would require a lot more resources. Nats + Jetstream will be used as the replacement. 

With this change, we now will be using a proper at-most-once queue for all messaging that should only be processed by a single replica of a single service. We will also be using a at-least-once fan out type queue for all broadcast communications to enable all connected websockets to push the proper notifications. 

Speed improvements were added as well. Transaction Group signing was a big issue. If the group was too large, and multiple keys were involved, the backend could not keep up. This causes a number of issues, including unresponsive UI, numerous emails/inapp notifications, etc. Now signing is done in a batched manner, all calls to the database are grouped were possible. 

**Related issue(s)**:

Fixes #1740 #1800 #1887 #1625 #1770 #1929 #1965 #1906

**Notes for reviewer**:
<!-- Provide logs, performance numbers or screenshots of the new functionality -->

**Checklist**

- [x] Documented (Code comments, README, etc.)
- [x] Tested (unit, integration, etc.)

items.title: fix: transactions table pagination is broken
items.body:
**Description**:
Changes below:
1) fix pagination in  transaction tables 
2) enable transaction grouping in `History` tab (when organization mode is enabled)

Those changes introduce a new API point in backend: `GET /transaction-nodes`.
Transaction node is a new entity which represents a single row in a transaction table.

More details in `Notes for reviewer` section


**Related issue(s)**:

Fixes #1626

**Notes for reviewer**:
```
N front-end/src/renderer/services/organization/transactionNode.ts
N back-end/apps/api/src/transactions/nodes/transaction-nodes.controller.ts
N back-end/apps/api/src/transactions/nodes/transaction-nodes.service.ts
N back-end/apps/api/src/transactions/nodes/transaction-nodes.util.ts
N back-end/apps/api/src/transactions/dto/transaction-node.dto.ts
U back-end/apps/api/src/transactions/dto/index.ts
U back-end/apps/api/src/transactions/transactions.module.ts
    # New back-end api point GET /transaction-nodes
    # Returns an array of ITransactionNode
    # Main parameter : collection (ie READY_TO_REVIEW, READY_TO_SIGN...)
    # Not paginated : all collection items are returned at once (to be revisited if needed)
    # TransactionNodesService.getTransactionNodes() is mainly a switch on existing routines
    #   - READY_FOR_REVIEW => TransactionsService.getTransactionsToApprove()
    #   - READY_TO_SIGN => TransactionsService.getTransactionsToSign()
    #   - READY_FOR_EXECUTION => TransactionsService.getTransactions(WAITING_FOR_EXECUTION)
    #   - IN_PROGRESS => TransactionsService.getTransactions(WAITING_FOR_SIGNATURES)
    #   - HISTORY => TransactionsService.getHistoryTransactions()


N middle-end/src/ITransactionNode.ts
N back-end/apps/api/src/transactions/dto/ITransactionNode.ts
U front-end/tsconfig.web.json
    # Interface of objects returned by back-end to front-end
    # Should be a single file located in middle-end/src folder
    # NestJS current setup cannot find files in middle-end folder :(

* sends the required headers from the front end when making requests with axios and ws

**Related issue(s)**:

**Notes for reviewer**:
Currently the `MINIMUM_SUPPORTED_FRONTEND_VERSION` is set to `0.21.0`

items.title: fix: Add logging framework & format to frontend
items.body:
## Add structured logging framework to the frontend

Fixes #2414 

### Problem

The frontend does not log anything. When users encounter problems there is no diagnostic trail they can share with developers, and developers cannot reproduce issues without guesswork. Every `console.log` scattered across the codebase is silently swallowed in production builds, so errors in websocket connections, transaction signing, database migrations, IPC calls, and version checks vanish without a trace.

### Why `electron-log` instead of `log4js`

The issue suggested evaluating `log4js` first. After investigation, `electron-log` was chosen because:

1. **Already available** — `electron-log` is a dependency of `electron-updater` (which the app already uses for auto-updates), so it introduces zero additional package overhead.
2. **Electron-native** — It understands Electron's process model out of the box (main, renderer, preload), handles `app.getPath('userData')` automatically, and integrates with Electron's crash reporter.
3. **Built-in rotation** — File rotation with configurable max size and archive count is included without additional plugins.
4. **Simpler IPC** — Renderer logs can be forwarded to the main process file transport through the existing preload bridge pattern, without needing a custom transport adapter that `log4js` would require in an Electron context.

### Solution

A structured JSON logging framework that writes to a single rotating log file with automatic sensitive-data sanitization.

**Architecture:**
- **Shared layer** (`shared/utils/logging.ts`) — log level types, argument normalization, key-based redaction, pattern scrubbing (JWT, Bearer, PEM), payload summarization, circular-reference detection, and depth-limited serialization.
- **Main process** (`main/modules/logger.ts`) — configures `electron-log` with a JSON file transport, log rotation (5 MB × 5 archives), console patching, and unhandled error capture. Exposes `createLogger(compone

items.title: feat: add frontend upgrade prompts based on version check response and failed guard
items.body:
**Description**:
Front end changes related to the version check and failing version check guard
New update modals are introduced:
* for mandatory updated when the current version is below the `minimumSupportedVersion` and when the guard is failing
* an optional one when `currentVersion >= minimumSupportedVersion` and `currentVersion < latestSupportedVersion`

The current flow is the following:
```
┌─────────────────┐
│   App Start     │
│   (Login Page)  │
└────────┬────────┘
         │
         ▼
┌─────────────────────────┐
│  User enters credentials│
│  Clicks Login           │
└────────┬────────────────┘
         │
         ▼
┌─────────────────────────┐
│  POST /auth/login       │
│  (goes through guard)   │
└────────┬────────────────┘
         │
    ┌────┴────────────────┐
    │                     │
    ▼                     ▼
Guard passes          Guard rejects
(version >= min)      (version < min)
    │                     │
    ▼                     ▼
Login succeeds        426 + updateUrl
    │                     │
    ▼                     ▼
Call version-check    Mandatory modal
    │                 (blocking, can't login)
    │
    ├── updateAvailable → Optional modal
    │
    └── current → No modal, continue
    
```

**Related issue(s)**:

Fixes https://github.com/hashgraph/hedera-transaction-tool/issues/2104

**Notes for reviewer**:
This PR includes changed from https://github.com/hashgraph/hedera-transaction-tool/pull/2129 
Should be rebased and merged after the other one.

items.title: Prompt users to enable email notifications after registration
items.body:
**Description:**
Since email notifications default to off, users need a way to discover and enable them. Add a prompt or in-app guidance directing users to their notification preferences after registration.

**Acceptance Tests:**
- New users are shown a prompt or instructional message indicating email notifications are off and where to enable them
- Prompt is dismissible and does not reappear after dismissal

items.title: Staging area for batching transaction notifications to signers
items.body:
**Description:**
When transactions are created one at a time, each triggers a separate email to council members. This becomes noisy at scale. A staging area would let DevOps queue up transactions without immediately notifying signers, then release them all at once with a single email — optionally with a name/description for the batch. This avoids disrupting the existing flow for single transactions.

**Notes:**
Open questions to resolve in design:
- How staging interacts with existing notification settings (opt-outs, etc.)
- Whether a batch is a flat group or supports nested grouping ("group in a group") — nested groups may be the right model if the desired UX is a single unified signing event
- Interval-based sending (e.g. hourly digest) as a simpler alternative worth evaluating against the staging approach

items.title: Notification badge count duplicates transaction events in lifecycle view
items.body:
**Description:**  
Notification badge counts are doubling the actual number of transaction events. For example, when a transaction expires, the badge shows two expired transactions while the history only reflects one. The same behavior occurs when transactions move to "ready for execution."

Navigating to another organization and back temporarily corrects the count, but subsequent transactions continue to be double-counted. The issue persists until the application is relaunched. This behavior was only observed in the first organization set up, not in a second organization using the same version. The issue has not been reproducible and has not been reported by other users.

This may be related to duplicate frontend event handling, such as multiple websocket listeners being registered.

**Expected behavior:**  
- Notification badge count accurately reflects the number of transaction events.  
- Each transaction event is counted only once.  
- Badge count remains consistent without requiring navigation or app restart.

**Acceptance criteria:**  
- Badge count matches the actual number of transaction events in history.  
- No duplicate counting occurs when transactions change state (e.g., expired, ready for execution).  
- Navigating between organizations does not affect badge accuracy.  
- No duplicate websocket listeners or event handlers are registered.

items.title: Fix transaction lifecycle notifications overriding "new" status
items.body:
**Description:**  
Transaction lifecycle notifications can override each other, causing users to miss new transactions. If a transaction requires 2-of-3 signatures and users B and C sign before user A sees it, user A loses the "new transaction" notification. The transaction moves silently from "new" to "ready to sign" without user A ever being notified.  

**Current behavior:**  
```
Tx requires 2/3 signatures (A,B,C)
→ B and C sign before A sees it
→ A sees: "Ready to sign" tab (no "new" notification)
→ A misses notification entirely
```

**Expected behavior:**  
```
Tx requires 2/3 signatures (A,B,C)
→ B and C sign before A sees it  
→ A still sees: "New transaction" notification
→ Notification persists until A acts on it (signs, dismisses, etc.)
```

**Root cause:**  
- Notification state transitions automatically (`new` → `ready to sign`)  
- Once overridden, "new" notification is lost even for eligible users  
- Users only see final state, not that they missed critical lifecycle events  

**Scope:**  
- Persist "new transaction" notification for eligible users until they **personally act** on it  
- Decouple notification state from transaction lifecycle state  
- Track per-user notification state independently  

**Acceptance criteria:**  
- [ ] Users see "new transaction" notification until they personally interact with it  
- [ ] Notification persists even if other users advance transaction state  
- [ ] 2-of-3 scenario: all 3 users see "new" notification until each acts  
- [ ] Notification clears only after user action (sign, dismiss) or final state (executed, expired, canceled)  
- [ ] "Ready to sign" notifications work independently of "new" notifications

items.title: Add user setting to opt out of beta release notifications
items.body:
**Description:**  
Currently all users are notified when a new beta release is available. We need a per-account setting so users can opt out of beta release notifications; when disabled, the user should no longer receive beta-only prompts while still receiving stable-release notices.

**Expected behavior:**  
- Toggling **“Join beta program”**, or similar, in Settings enables/disables all beta release notifications and update prompts for that user.  
- Stable-release notifications remain unchanged.

**Scope:**  
- Add a per-user boolean preference (Settings UI + API + DB).  
- Make notification paths (frontend, backend, email/automation) respect the preference.  

**Acceptance criteria:**  
- User can toggle the setting and it persists.  
- Opted-out users do not receive beta notifications across channels; opted-in users still do.  
- Tests cover persistence and enforcement.

items.title: Add email notification for archived transactions
items.body:
**Description**:
When a transaction is archived, send an email notification to all *interested parties* by default.  

- Default recipients:
  - Creator  
  - Signer  
  - Observer  
  - Approver  
- The email should include:
  - Transaction ID and title  
  - Archive timestamp  
  - User who performed the archive action

items.title: Investigate stale “signature required” notifications after execution
items.body:
**Description**:  
Users report seeing notifications that still indicate signatures are required even after the transaction has already been executed, and they never signed the transaction. In the database, the `notification_receiver` entities for that user/notification/type are already gone, suggesting the notification was cleared server-side but the UI did not update. There may be an issue where a status-change event (e.g., to `EXECUTED`) was not sent or not handled correctly, even though there are no stuck messages in NATS.

**Requirements**:  
- Investigate the notification lifecycle for transactions moving to `EXECUTED`, especially when the user has not signed.  
- Verify that:
  - The backend emits the appropriate notification-clear / status-update event when a transaction executes.  
  - The client subscribes to and correctly handles that event to remove or update the notification.    

**Notes**:
I do not know if a hard restart clears the notifications, nor how long the app was open before use. It could be a websocket issue, where the user received the notification, slept the machine long enough for the notifications to come and and clear NATS, then reopened the machine and now notifications didn't repull/auto clear.

items.title: Clicking notifications does not refresh Transactions UI
items.body: ### Description When receiving transactions to sign, a notification UI will pop up. If you are working on a different network, this will change to the network that the notification was for. However the UI, in particular the "Ready to sign" tab, will not be updated. ### Steps to reproduce 1. Create a backend 2. As a transaction signer, Select 'mainnet' as the backend. Click on **Ready to Sign** 3. Observe 0 transanctions ready to sign 3. As a transaction creator, submit transaction(s) to 'custom' network (or any network other than 'mainnet') 4. You will receive notifications for these new transaction(s). Click on the notifications 5. EXPECT: you will see transaction(s) to sign 6. OBSERVE: no transactions to sign, though a red number for # of notifications appear 7. Clicking another tab (e.g **Ready for Review**) forces the transactions to appear ### Additional context _No response_ ### Hedera network _No response_ ### Version v0.24.0 ### Operating system macOS

items.title: Websocket Notification for Contact List Refresh
items.body:
### Problem
- No websocket notification is sent when a new user is invited to the organization.
- Connected users on the contact list page don't receive real-time updates about new organization members.

### Proposed Solution
- When an admin invites a new user, broadcast a websocket notification to all connected users in the organization.
- The notification should trigger a contact list refresh only for users currently viewing the contact list page.
- Include the new user's data in the notification payload when possible.

### Acceptance Criteria
- Admin invites new user → websocket notification sent to all connected org users.
- Users on contact list page receive notification and refresh their list to show new user.
- Users on other pages receive notification but don't trigger unnecessary refreshes.

items.title: Creator should receive transaction created notifications
items.body: User Feedback: A creator should receive the same transaction inapp and email notifications as any signer would receive. The main reason for this feedback is so that the creator will have more confidence that the notifications were sent.

items.title: Local notification storage on app reset
items.body: I had my app set up, connected to different orgs. I had some notifications in my notifications center. I reset the app, closed and relaunched the app. I did not reconnect to any organization, just set up the personal mode. My notifications center still showed notifications from the previously connected orgs (showed the org url, instead of just the org nickname).

items.title: User Registered Notification not routing user correctly
items.body:
I have 2 orgs (in addition to Personal). I was in org1, but org2 sent me a notification that a user had finished registration. I clicked the notification, and was routed to the Contact List correctly. The app did not, however, switch me to the proper org. 

These org switching notification actions do appear to work correctly with other notification types, like expired transactions, etc.

items.title: Account setup notification on different org not redirecting
items.body:
If the user receives a notification from an organization that is not currently selected, clicking on the notification doesn't appear to properly redirect the user to the org and network. This is the case for contact registration, and perhaps other cases.

1. Connect to an organization.
2. Invite a new user to the org.
3. Switch to Personal (might require 2 organizations, but this might be sufficient)
4. Have the user complete registration.
5. Click on the notification once received.

items.title: Upgrade Module When Triggered by Non-Active Org

When working on a task for org x, my work is interupted with an alert module saying a non-org x update is required.  Given the alert is unrelated my current task, I would like the notification to go to the bell notifications vs. an module.

As a user, I'd like a smooth and streamlined experience when interacting with the app.

items.title: Add config/env flag to disable all email notifications globally
items.body:
**Description:**
Add a config/env option to disable the email notification system entirely for all users. Transactional emails (registration, password reset, etc.) must remain unaffected by this flag.

**Acceptance Tests:**
- When the flag is set, no non-transactional notification emails are sent to any user
- Transactional emails continue to send regardless of the flag's value
- Flag defaults to enabled so existing deployments are unaffected

items.title: Revise email notification system to reduce per-transaction noise
items.body:
**Description:**
The email system can fire individual notifications for each transaction in a group, which generates excessive volume — e.g. a group of 10 transactions staggered by seconds produces 10 separate emails per user per lifecycle event (created, signed, executed, expired). Notifications should be scoped to the group lifecycle or batched by time window.

**Acceptance Tests:**
- Transactions that belong to a group do not trigger per-transaction emails for events that have a group-level equivalent (created, ready to execute, executed, expired)
- Group lifecycle emails correctly reflect aggregate state (e.g. "all transactions signed, group ready to execute")
- Solo transactions (no group) continue to receive standard per-transaction notifications, unaffected
- A batching/digest option exists for high-frequency events (e.g. daily summary of executed transactions)

**Notes:**
Two viable approaches worth evaluating before implementation: (1) group-scoped notifications keyed to group lifecycle events, (2) time-windowed digests (hourly or daily) for certain event types. These are not mutually exclusive.

items.title: Importing UI/UX improvements
items.body:
The process of importing transactions and signatures, from either TTv1 or TTv2, needs to be redesigned in order to be more visually intuitive. The current design is strong and effective, but lacks the tools that will help the user clearly see what transactions will be altered, and how they will be altered. 

It was discussed to use the Notification feature to help the user know which transactions were changed, and direct the user to the proper page. As the response from the server is received via the API request, and not the websocket, this solution wouldn't actually require the notification table in the database. This solution can all be done locally and temporarily, only lasting for the current session.

Other solutions should be explored as well.

items.title: Contact list not updating after new user invitation
items.body:
## Description
When an admin invites a new user to the organization, the new user does not appear in the contact list for any users. Manual navigation doesn't refresh the list, and no websocket notification is sent to trigger updates.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Tests need to be added to ensure no future regressions

items.title: Closing the application does not fully close it
items.body:
### Problem

After closing the transaction tool I still see 3 transaction tool processes in activity monitor. This may appear to users that the app is either buggy and didn't close properly or is spying on it in the background.  In reality, I imagine this is for some background processes to do notifications or to sync transactions. Even so, I prefer to not waste compute resources on an app that I run once or twice a month to sign a transaction.

### Solution

Closing the app should terminate all related processes. Any notifications could use an external medium like email, slack, apple push messages, etc.

### Alternatives

An option to soft close vs hard close.
An option in the settings whether close should

items.title: Error Messaging Audit & Enhancement
items.body:
**Problem Statement:** Our current error messages often leave users confused about what went wrong and how to resolve issues, leading to poor user experience and increased support burden.

**Objective**: Conduct a comprehensive audit of all user-facing error messages to ensure they are:

- Clear and understandable to users of all technical levels
- Actionable with specific next steps
- Consistent in tone and format across the application

Scope:

All user-facing error messages throughout the application
API error responses
Validation messages
System failure notifications
Success Criteria: Each error message should include:

1. What happened - Clear explanation of the error
2. Why it happened - Context when helpful and not technical
3. What to do next - Specific actionable steps (e.g., "Retry in 5 minutes," "Check your network connection," "Contact support with error code XYZ")

items.title: Enable TT to refresh contract list when contacts are created/deleted on another TT
items.body:
### Problem

This is a follow-up of #2108.

TT should be extended to support the two computers scenario described in #2108.

```
1) I started TT on two computers A and B
2) I connected those two TT on the same backend
    => both TT display the same contact list
3) On computer A, I created a new contact
    => on computer A, Contact List panel displays the new contact
    => on computer B, Contact List panel does not show the new contact
      => navigating back and forth does not show the new contact either
      => in browser dev tools, no websocket activity related to contact/user are reported
      => after restarting TT, Contact List shows the new contact
```

### Solution

Add the required notifications that will tell compute B to refresh its contact list.

### Alternatives

_No response_

items.title: Update backend documentation for possible TLS termination issues
items.body:
Every backend setup may be different. In each case, it is necessary that the api pod knows the protocol used for the connection. If the ingress, or some other outside redirect causes the protocol to not be passed on (or due to TLS termination), then the api won't have the proper information that it needs. 

Update the backend documentation to include a warning that automated emails require knowledge of the protocol used for the connection. In the case of TLS termination, this requires forwarded the protocol used to the ingress/pod. Without this, the api and notification services may display the incorrect protocol for the backend.

items.title: Remote backends cannot execute against local node or local solo deployments
items.body: Transactions that are submitted to a remote organization's backend cannot be executed against a localnode or a local solo deployment. This is expected. The frontend should prevent the user from attempted to create a transaction with this configuration.

items.title: Signers do not receive notifications after signing transaction
items.body:
**Description:**  
Users who sign a transaction are not receiving notifications for subsequent lifecycle events, such as when the transaction becomes ready for execution or when it has been executed. Once a user signs a transaction, notifications for that transaction stop entirely.

**Expected behavior:**  
- Signers continue to receive notifications for all relevant transaction lifecycle events after signing.  
- Notifications include states such as ready for execution, executed, and other applicable updates.

**Acceptance criteria:**  
- Signers receive notifications after signing a transaction.  
- Notifications are triggered for all relevant lifecycle state changes.  
- Verified by signing a transaction and confirming notifications for subsequent events.

items.title: Suppress In-App Update Notification When Running Version Meets Minimum Version Threshold
items.body:
**Summary**
Currently, the application displays an update notification message whenever a version difference is detected across connected organizations. This results in unnecessary noise for users who are running a version that already meets or exceeds the minimum required version. This change suppresses the update notification in those cases, surfacing it only when the running version falls below the minimum required version of a connected organization.

**Current Behavior**
When a version difference is detected between the locally running instance and any connected organization, an in-app update message is displayed regardless of whether the current version is sufficient to operate with those organizations.

**Expected Behavior**

If the locally running version is equal to or greater than the minimum required version across all connected organizations, the update notification is suppressed.

If the locally running version is below the minimum required version of one or more connected organizations, the update notification is displayed as it is today.  No other changes to the notification content, style, or dismissal behavior are in scope.

items.title: Settings tab only shows organization-specific settings instead of app-wide settings
items.body:
## Description
The current Settings tab is scoped only to the connected organization, which is confusing for users. When users open Settings, they expect to see all settings relevant to the app as a whole, but instead they only see settings for the currently connected organization. This makes it unclear where to find global app settings and blurs the distinction between app-level and organization-level configuration. 

## Problem
- Settings currently shows only organization-specific options tied to the active organization.
- Users expect a Settings area to contain global app settings (e.g., account, security, appearance, general behavior), not just organization-level configuration.
- Users do not have a clear place to manage organization details separately from app-level configuration.

## Expected Behavior
- The Settings tab should be the central place for app-wide settings.
- Users should be able to quickly see and manage:
- Global app settings (account, security, general preferences, etc.).
- A clear entry point to organization-specific settings/details.
- Organization-specific configuration should be clearly separated from global settings so users understand the scope of each change.

## Actual Behavior
- The Settings tab primarily exposes settings for the currently connected organization.
- Users become confused when they open Settings and only see organization settings, assuming they are looking at all app settings.
- There is no dedicated, clearly labeled place for per-organization details/settings distinct from global app-level configuration.

## Proposed UX / Structural Changes
1. Make the Settings tab the home for app-wide/global settings:
    - Show user/account, security, general app behavior, notifications, etc.
    - Treat this as the “App Settings” area rather than “Organization Settings”.
2. Move organization-specific settings into an Organization Details section/page:
    - Accessible from within Settings (e.g., “Organizations” → select an organizat

items.title: Add missing notification preferences and improve notification settings UI
items.body:
**Description**:  
The email notification settings page is missing a couple of opt-in/out options and the layout is not following a common, easy-to-scan pattern. We need to add preferences for “Executed” and “Expired” transaction emails, and update the UI to use a clearer grouped layout with concise labels and descriptions.

**Requirements**:  
- Add new notification preference toggles for:
  - Transactions executed  
  - Transactions expired  
- Ensure each preference maps correctly to the corresponding notification type on the backend.  
- Update the UI to a more standard pattern:
  - Section heading (e.g., “Email notifications”) with a short intro line.  
  - Indented list of options, one per row, with:
    - A short state label (e.g., “Ready to execute”, “Signature required”, “Cancelled”, “Executed”, “Expired”).  
    - A right-aligned toggle.  
    - A brief description underneath explaining when an email is sent (e.g., “The transaction has enough signatures and can be executed.”).  
- Ensure attributes are set.

items.title: Make notification services resilient to NATS outages
items.body:
**Description**:  
`api/chain/notification` services should not crash or become unavailable if NATS is down or unreachable. They must continue to function, and a backup solution for fan-out messaging (e.g., TCP-based or similar) should be researched and implemented so that notifications can still be delivered when NATS is unavailable.

**Requirements**:  
- Update NATS client usage so that:
  - Service startup does not crash if NATS cannot be reached.  
  - Connection loss triggers graceful degradation instead of process exit (e.g., stop publishing to NATS, but keep HTTP/API endpoints up).
- Implement reconnection and health-handling logic:
  - Use automatic reconnection with backoff and unlimited retries where appropriate.
  - Log NATS connection errors and status changes clearly for operations.
- Research and design a backup fan-out mechanism when NATS is unavailable:
  - Options could include direct TCP connections, database-backed outbox pattern, or another message transport that can be enabled as a fallback.
  - Define how messages are queued and delivered when NATS returns.  
- Define behavior for fan-out when NATS is down:
  - How to avoid message loss.  
  - How to avoid duplicate delivery when NATS comes back and both paths might send.  

**Acceptance criteria**:  
- `api/chain/notification` services remain up and responsive when NATS is unavailable (no crashes or failed container restarts).  
- NATS outages are handled via reconnection logic and clear logs, without blocking core APIs.
- A documented backup fan-out design is agreed upon and an initial implementation is in place (or a concrete plan exists if phased).  
- Fan-out behavior is verified under simulated NATS outage (e.g., stopping the NATS cluster) and recovery, with no data loss and no service crashes.

items.title: Observers to be used for group notifications
items.body:
Popular apps like Slack allow for another avenue for notifications. Users have mentioned that they don't check emails as frequently as they check Slack. In order to notify people as quickly as possible, there needs to be a way to add a specific email to a transaction that will receive these notifications (if I understand correctly). 

Once the observer feature is fully in place, the admin can add a Slack user that will then be added to the transactions as an observer and facilitate the Slack notification request.

items.title: TransactionDetails stepper renders stale status on mount
items.body:
### Description

On the transaction detail page, status-changing WebSocket events that fire before TransactionDetails.vue mounts are silently dropped, because the useWebsocketSubscription(TRANSACTION_ACTION, …) subscriber only exists while the component is mounted. The cached backend transaction therefore stays at its pre-event status, and the stepper renders that stale value on first paint.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Be on a list view (e.g. Ready to Sign) when an upstream actor signs/executes a transaction such that a TRANSACTION_ACTION WS event fires while the detail page is not mounted.
2. Open the transaction detail page.
3. Observe: stepper shows the prior status (e.g. "Waiting for signatures") even though the backend now reports the new status (e.g. "Executed").
4. Forcing a refetch (navigating away and back, or invalidating the cache) clears the stale render.

### Additional context

Affected file                                                                                                                                                                
- `front-end/src/renderer/pages/TransactionDetails/TransactionDetails.vue `— onBeforeMount currently calls `fetchTransaction()`, which reads from the cache and skips invalidation.
                                                                                                                                                                               
Proposed fix                                                                                                                                                                 
- Invalidate the cached entry once on mount before the first fetch — the same `fetchTransactionOnNotif()` path the WS handler already uses:                                      
                                                                                                                                                                               
```
  onBeforeMount(async () => {

items.title: Clear notifications on sign using API response, not websocket
items.body:
**Description**:  
After signing a transaction, if the transaction is marked "unread", the backend currently marks it as read and sends a "delete notification" message over the websocket. Multiple users report that their notifications are not being removed when they sign. We should not rely on a websocket push to clear notifications; the UI should clear notifications based on the API response from the sign action so it is more responsive and less brittle.

**Requirements**:  
- Update the sign-transaction flow so that the API response explicitly indicates that the related notification should be cleared (e.g., include a flag or updated notification list/state).  
- Update the frontend to:
  - Clear the notification immediately based on the successful sign API response, where the response includes the "delete notification" message.  
  - Remove the websocket "delete notification" message after signing.  
- Add logging/telemetry around notification clearing to help confirm behavior in production.

**Acceptance criteria**:  
- After successfully signing a transaction, the related notification is cleared immediately in the UI using only the API response.  
- Users no longer observe stale notifications remaining after sign.

items.title: Simplify email content and remove detailed transaction data
items.body:
**Description**:  
Recent feedback on the new email design suggests that listing individual transactions (especially in large numbers) is not very helpful, and including detailed data in emails may introduce security and privacy risks. Update notification emails to be simpler and more high-level, describing the type and meaning of the notification without embedding transaction details.

**Requirements**:  
- Review all notification email templates that currently include transaction lists or detailed transaction data.  
- Simplify email bodies to:
  - Describe the event type and what it means (e.g., “You have transactions ready to execute”, “Some transactions have expired”).  
  - Avoid listing individual transaction IDs, amounts, or other sensitive details.  
  - Encourage users to open the app to see full details (e.g., “View details in the Hedera Transaction Tool”).

items.title: Account Create Transaction with Complex key is not working on Testnet
items.body:
### Description

When creating an account using a Complex Key, the transaction is not consistently propagated to all associated signers. Specifically, one (or more) of the expected signers does not receive the transaction request, preventing proper signature collection and blocking successful completion of the account creation process.

### Steps to reproduce

Initiate an Account Create Transaction
Assign a Complex Key with multiple public keys (multiple signers)
Submit the transaction for signing
Observe signer notifications / transaction requests

Expected Result:

All signers associated with the Complex Key should receive the transaction request
Each signer should be able to review and sign the transaction

Actual Result:

One of the signers does not receive the transaction request
The transaction cannot be fully signed and completed

Impact:

Blocks account creation when Complex Keys are used
Breaks multi-signature workflow reliability
May cause confusion for users expecting all parties to receive the request

Notes / Additional Context:

Issue appears specific to Complex Key usage (multi-key setup)
Needs verification whether this is consistent or intermittent

Suggested Investigation Areas:

Transaction propagation logic for multi-signature flows
Signer notification / delivery mechanism
Mapping between Complex Key public keys and signer identities

### Additional context

_No response_

### Hedera network

testnet

### Version

v0.27.0

### Operating system

macOS

items.title: Notifications service errors when creator key has been deleted
items.body:
**Description**  
When the notifications service attempts to calculate which users are affected by a transaction status change, it fails if the creator key has been deleted. In this scenario, the service tries to look up the user ID associated with the deleted creator key and throws an error. Since this logic is only used to notify users, a missing/deleted creator key should not cause the process to fail.

**Expected behavior**  
- When the creator key for a transaction has been deleted, the notifications service should skip that user and continue processing other recipients.  
- No errors should be thrown solely due to a deleted creator key during the “affected users” calculation.

**Actual behavior**  
- The notifications service attempts to resolve the user ID for the deleted creator key.  
- This lookup fails and results in an error, interrupting the notification flow.

**Steps to reproduce**  
1. Create a transaction with a given creator key.  
2. Delete that creator key from the system.  
3. Trigger a status change on the transaction that would normally send notifications.  
4. Observe that the notifications service throws an error when resolving the creator’s user ID.

**Proposed solution**  
- Update the notifications service logic so that, when it cannot resolve a user ID for the creator key (because the key is deleted or missing), it logs the situation and excludes that user from the notification recipients instead of throwing an error.

items.title: Regression: signatures do not appear until relaunch on v0.29.0
items.body:
**Description:**  
**User1** was using v0.27.0 and **User2** was using v0.29.0. In that setup, **User1** could see signatures from **User2**, but **User2** could not see signatures from **User1** until relaunching the app. After **User1** updated to v0.29.0, neither user could see the other person’s signatures until relaunch. This points to a regression in live signature updates between v0.27.0 and v0.29.0.

**Expected behavior:**  
When one user signs a transaction, the other user should see the signature update immediately without needing to relaunch.

**Actual behavior:**  
With **User1** on v0.27.0 and **User2** on v0.29.0, signature updates were only visible in one direction. After **User1** updated to v0.29.0, signature updates were no longer visible in either direction until relaunch.

**Notes:**  
- The issue should be investigated across v0.27.0, v0.28.0, and v0.29.0.
- Existing tests should be updated, or a new test should be added, to cover this behavior and prevent regressions in the future.
- Both notification pushes as well as selecting the affected transactions resulted in no updates.

items.title: Improve signing flow and error handling
items.body:
**Description:**  
Update the signing flow so **Sign & Next** only advances after a successful signing operation. Replace the generic **failed to connect** error with a more actionable error message and add logging that provides enough detail to diagnose signing failures.

**Requirements:**  
- Ensure **Sign & Next** only proceeds after signing succeeds.  
- Replace the generic **failed to connect** message with a clearer, more actionable error.  
- Add logging that helps identify why signing failed.  
- Add tests or verification for the failure path.

**Acceptance criteria:**  
- The UI does not advance unless signing completes successfully.  
- Users see a useful error when signing fails.  
- Logging provides enough detail to troubleshoot the failure.  
- The signing flow is covered by tests or reproducible validation.

items.title: Do not broadcast cache refresh notifications when no data changed
items.body:
**Description**  
The mirror node currently refreshes several caches on a fixed schedule (e.g., every N seconds/minutes). After each scheduled refresh it sends a “refresh” notification to all connected users/clients, instructing them to reload their data, even when the underlying cache data has not changed since the previous refresh. This causes unnecessary client updates and wasted network usage.

We need to ensure that refresh notifications are only sent when the cache content actually changes.

**Acceptance Criteria**

- When a scheduled cache refresh runs and the cache data remains identical to its previous state:
  - No refresh notification is emitted to users/clients.
- When a scheduled cache refresh detects any change in the cached data (new items, removed items, or updated values):
  - A refresh notification is emitted to all relevant connected users/clients.

items.title: Notifications still not reliably cleared after signing from “Ready to sign” tab
items.body:
**Description**  
This is a follow‑up to issue https://github.com/hashgraph/hedera-transaction-tool/issues/2319. The original fix improved notification clearing when signing from the “Ready to sign” tab, but it does not fully address the problem. In particular, if the user interacts quickly with the UI (for example, signing and immediately navigating) or if notification refreshes occur while the signing operation is in progress, the “new transaction ready to sign” notification is not always cleared. This results in transactions remaining flagged as “new” or “ready to sign” even after the user has successfully signed and there is no further action required from them.

**Steps to reproduce**  
1. Create multiple multisig transactions that will appear in the “Ready to sign” tab in status “waiting for signatures”.  
2. Open the “Ready to sign” tab and ensure that “new transaction ready to sign” notifications are visible for these transactions.  
3. Attempt to sign all of the transactions quickly in succession from the “Ready to sign” tab.  
4. Observe the incorrect badge number indicating new transactions on the “Ready to sign” tab.

**Expected behavior**  
- After the user signs each transaction from the “Ready to sign” tab, the “new transaction ready to sign” notification for that transaction should reliably be cleared for that user, even when signing many transactions quickly.  
- Notification state should accurately reflect whether the user has any remaining signing actions for each transaction.

**Actual behavior**  
- When signing multiple transactions quickly from the “Ready to sign” tab, some “new transaction ready to sign” notifications are not cleared.  
- The user continues to see stale notifications for transactions they have already signed and cannot act on, which is confusing and makes it harder to identify truly new items that require attention.

items.title: Notifications not cleared when navigating between transactions or viewing new creator-signed transactions
items.body:
**Description**  
When navigating between transactions using “Next/Prev” or “Sign & next”, notifications for the transactions being viewed are not cleared, even when they should be marked as read for the current user. In addition, when a creator submits a transaction that also requires the creator’s signature, the transaction details are shown immediately and are signable. If the creator does not sign at that time but navigates back to the Transactions tab, the new transaction still appears as “new”, even though the creator already viewed its details. This makes it harder to distinguish truly new or unseen items. It looks like many, but not all, “mark as read” calls could be consolidated into a single, central place (for example, whenever the transaction details view is opened) so that any transaction the user has viewed no longer shows a “new” notification for that user.

**Steps to reproduce**  
1. Open a transaction list that shows “new” or similar notifications for transactions that require attention.  
2. Open a transaction’s details.  
3. Use “Next/Prev” or “Sign & next” to move between transactions that currently have notifications.  
4. Return to the transaction list and observe notifications for the transactions that were viewed via navigation.  
5. As a separate scenario, create a transaction that also requires the creator’s own signature.  
6. After the transaction is created and its details view is shown, do not sign it; instead, navigate back to the Transactions tab.  
7. Observe how the newly created transaction is still marked as “new” for the creator.

**Expected behavior**  
- When a transaction’s details are viewed (including when reached via “Next/Prev” or “Sign & next”), any “new” notification for that transaction should be cleared for the current user.  
- When a creator submits a transaction that they immediately view in the details screen, that transaction should no longer appear as “new” once they leave the details view, even if they did not

items.title: Investigate receiver_queue_worker NATS consumer timeouts and unprocessed notification messages
items.body:
**Description**  
The NATS consumer `receiver_queue_worker` is failing to process messages for at least the subjects `notifications.queue.transaction.status-update` and `notifications.queue.transaction.update`. After attempting to process the current notifications, the consumer times out and is removed. In contrast, the `email_queue_worker` never times out and continues to wait for messages that should be produced by `receiver_queue_worker`, causing the notification flow to stall.

We need to diagnose why `receiver_queue_worker` is not successfully processing or acknowledging these messages, and why the consumer is timing out and being deleted, then fix the underlying issues so that messages on these subjects are handled reliably.

**Acceptance Criteria**

- Root cause is identified for:
  - Why `receiver_queue_worker` is failing to process messages on `notifications.queue.transaction.status-update` and `notifications.queue.transaction.update` (e.g., deserialization errors, handler exceptions, ack logic, subscription configuration, or subject mismatch).
  - Why the NATS consumer for `receiver_queue_worker` is timing out and being removed instead of remaining healthy or recovering.
- `receiver_queue_worker`:
  - Reliably consumes and processes messages from the affected subjects under normal load.
  - Acknowledges messages correctly (or negatively acknowledges them with appropriate retries) so they are not stuck or repeatedly causing timeouts.
  - Logs clear errors when a message cannot be processed, without causing the consumer to be removed.
- The NATS consumer configuration (timeouts, max delivery, idle heartbeat, etc., if applicable) is reviewed and adjusted as needed so the worker does not time out and get deleted under expected operating conditions.
- `email_queue_worker` correctly receives messages or signals from `receiver_queue_worker` once processing succeeds, and no longer waits indefinitely due to upstream failures.
- Add or update automated tests or inte

items.title: Signing from “Ready to sign” tab does not clear “new transaction ready to sign” notification
items.body:
**Description**  
When I sign a multisig transaction from the “Ready to sign” tab, the “new transaction ready to sign” notification is not cleared if my signature alone is not enough to move the transaction from “waiting for signatures” to “waiting for execution”. This leaves a persistent notification for a transaction that I have already signed and can no longer act on, which is confusing and makes it harder to see truly new transactions that still need my attention.

**Steps to reproduce**  
1. Have a multisig transaction in status “waiting for signatures”.  
2. Observe that the “new transaction ready to sign” notification is shown.  
3. Open the “Ready to sign” tab.  
4. Sign the transaction, but do not add enough signatures to move the transaction status from “waiting for signatures” to “waiting for execution”.

**Expected behavior**  
After I sign the transaction from the “Ready to sign” tab, the “new transaction ready to sign” notification for that transaction should be cleared for my account, even if the overall transaction status remains “waiting for signatures”.

**Actual behavior**  
After signing from the “Ready to sign” tab, the “new transaction ready to sign” notification remains visible, even though I have already signed and there is nothing left for me to do on that transaction.

items.title: Emails failed to send on a production environment
items.body:
## Notification System Fails When Transaction Requires Deleted User's Key

### Description
When a transaction is created that requires a key belonging to a soft-deleted user, the notification system creates a `NotificationReceiver` entity but fails when attempting to send the email notification. This occurs because TypeORM filters out soft-deleted users, causing the notification process to fail.

### Steps to Reproduce
1. User A has a key assigned to them
2. User A is deleted (soft delete)
3. Create a transaction that requires User A's key
4. Share the transaction to the backend
5. Backend creates `NotificationReceiver` entity for the deleted user
6. System attempts to send email notification
7. **Error:** TypeORM doesn't return the soft-deleted user
8. Notification system fails

### Root Cause
The notification system identifies required signers based on keys without checking if the key owner has been deleted. TypeORM's soft delete mechanism then prevents the user from being retrieved during the email sending process, causing a failure.

### Proposed Solutions

#### Option 1: Filter Keys by User Status
When creating notifications based on required keys, verify that each key belongs to a non-deleted user before creating the `NotificationReceiver` entity.

**Pros:**
- Maintains existing key data
- Minimal database changes
- Clear separation of concerns

**Cons:**
- Requires additional query or join during notification creation
- Deleted user's keys remain in the system

#### Option 2: Cascade Delete Keys
When a user is deleted, also delete (or soft-delete) all keys associated with that user.

**Pros:**
- Cleaner data model
- Prevents orphaned keys
- Simpler notification logic

**Cons:**
- May impact audit trail if keys need to be retained for historical records
- Requires migration strategy for existing deleted users

### Implementation Considerations
- [ ] Decide on solution approach (Option 1, 2, or hybrid)
- [ ] Determine if soft-deleted user keys should be retaine

items.title: Refactor Backend Notification System
items.body:
The notification system, as implemented, on the backend is not actually scalable. Currently, all messaging sent from one service to another is fire-and-forget, and is only processed by a single replica. This means that when api sends anything to be processed by chain, RabbitMQ only allows 1 chain service to get the message and process it. Same thing with the notification service.

**Issues with design**
With the current design, there is no acknowledgement that the consumer of the message actually consumes the message. In addition, in app notifications need to be fanned out to all connected users. It works for now due to all backend deployments using a single notification replica. If more are provisioned, it will no longer work.

**Solution**
All consumers should acknowledge, always. Whether manually acknowledged (for EventPattern where RabbitMQ needs to know), or automatically (for MessagePattern where the producer is waiting), we don't want any messages to get lost.  
Email and InApp notifications will need to be separated into different queues. Email can be a simple queue, like it currently is. But InApp notifications needs to be a queue coupled with a fanout exchange. This means that a lot of the notification service code will need to be separated out, and the notification logic will need to be redesigned to handle these notifications separately.

items.title: Manual 'Sign Transaction' reminder notification does not clear from notification popup
items.body: I created a transaction, then shared it with the org. I then manually clicked 'Remind Signers'. Now that signer has a notification in the notification popup that won't go away even when clicked. The database shows it to be like any other notification, but isRead is false, and they type is 'TRANSACTION_WAITING_FOR_SIGNATURES_REMINDER_MANUAL'. The notification should appear the same as if it were the original notification. It should replace the original notification, if it still exists, so both notifications don't show up in the app. Once the notification is clicked (or the transaction is viewed) the notification should be removed, just like the original notification sent to signers.

**Description:**  
The history page no longer properly displays notifications or marks them as read when viewed within an organization. The history page of an organization uses the new `TransactionNodeTable` not the History.vue page.  

**Steps to Reproduce:**  
1. View the history page for an organization that uses `TransactionNodeTable`.  
2. Observe that:  
   - Notifications are not accurately displayed in the tab’s badge count.  
   - Newly received notifications are not highlighted.  
   - Notifications remain unread unless manually clicked.  

**Expected Behavior:**  
- The history page should correctly display the number of notifications in the tab badge.  
- Newly received notifications should be visually highlighted (e.g., highlighted rows using our current format).  
- All notifications visible on the history page should be automatically marked as “read” when the tab is opened, without requiring the user to click each one individually.

items.title: Improve cache refresh error handling for offline mirror nodes
items.body:
**Description:**  
Cache refreshing doesn't handle offline/unavailable mirror nodes gracefully. When a mirror node goes down, the current error handling blocks processing and produces verbose/less-useful logs. The system should treat mirror node failures as non-blocking, log them succinctly, and potentially implement backoff per offending network.  

**Current behavior:**  
```
Mirror node X offline → Cache refresh blocks → Processing stalls → Verbose error spam in logs
```

**Expected behavior:**  
```
Mirror node X offline → Non-blocking error → Succinct log entry → Continue processing other nodes
→ Optional: Backoff retry for network X specifically
```

**Scope:**  
- Make mirror node failures **non-blocking** (continue processing other nodes)  
- Improve error logging to be more succinct/actionable  
- Track failures per network/mirror node for backoff logic  
- Ensure cache refresh failures don't halt transaction processing  

**Acceptance criteria:**  
- [ ] Mirror node timeout/offline doesn't block cache refresh for other nodes  
- [ ] Cache refresh failures are logged succinctly (network + node + error type)  
- [ ] Processing continues immediately despite individual node failures  
- [ ] Per-network backoff tracking prevents log spam from repeated failures  
- [ ] Notification/messaging processing is never blocked by cache refresh errors  

**Nice to have:**  
- Circuit breaker per mirror node/network    
- Metrics for mirror node health/failure rates

items.title: Notify users on cache table updates via websocket
items.body:
## Description

When the cron job updates an account or node in the `cached_account` or `cached_node` tables, send a websocket notification to all connected users via `notifyClients()`. This triggers a client-side data refresh to reflect the latest cached data.

## Requirements

- In the cron job refresh logic, after successfully updating a record in:
  - `cached_account`
  - `cached_node`
- Leverage the notification system to broadcast a notification to all connected users (`notifyClients()`). 
- Notification payload should include:
  - userIds of all users that own the keys belonging to the updated entities (notifyClients() does not current make full use of these userIds, but will in the future).
- Clients receiving the notification refresh their local transaction lists or relevant UI data.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Cron job updates a cached account/node → websocket notification sent via `notifyClients()`.
- All connected users receive the notification with correct entity details.
- Clients handle the notification by refreshing relevant data (transaction lists, etc.).
- No notification sent on failed updates or `lastCheckedAt` updates without data changes.
- Tests verify notification is triggered only on successful data updates in cache tables.

Scope:

All user-facing error messages throughout the application

Validation messages
System failure notifications

1. What happened - Clear explanation of the error
2. Why it happened - Context when helpful and not technical

items.title: Resolve transaction group signing performance bottlenecks
items.body:
**Description**:  
Multiple users reported significant slowdowns when signing large transaction groups from the "Ready to Sign" page, experiencing 3-5 minute wait times for completion. Postgres showed all database connections in the pool exhausted during these operations, suggesting potential connection leaks or insufficient pool capacity. Investigate connection management, optimize signing throughput, and ensure scalable performance for group signing operations.

**Reported symptoms:**
- Signing large transaction groups takes 3-5 minutes instead of expected seconds.
- Observed: All Postgres connections in pool were in use during signing operations.
- Users affected: Multiple independent reports from the "Ready to Sign" page.

**Investigation areas:**
- **Database connection management**:
  - Verify no connections remain open after signing operations complete.
  - Audit transaction signing code for proper connection handling (no unclosed cursors, transactions, or sessions).
  - Check for long-running queries blocking the pool during bulk signing.
- **Signing operation efficiency**:
  - Profile individual transaction signing performance at scale.
  - Identify if sequential processing creates bottlenecks vs. parallel opportunities.
  - Review query patterns for signing updates (batch vs individual updates).
- **Pool capacity**:
  - Measure peak connection usage during group signing.
  - Document current pool size vs observed demand.

**Acceptance criteria:**
- Signing 100+ transaction groups completes in <60 seconds (target: <10s).
- Database connection pool never exceeds 80% utilization during peak signing.
- No connections remain open >2 minutes.
- Signing progress shows real-time updates (N of M signed) to users.
- Load test: 5 concurrent users signing 100-tx groups simultaneously succeeds.

items.title: Show client version and update availability for users in contact list
items.body:
Admin feedback: The old tool would output a file indicating when a user had upgraded to a newer version of the tool. This functionality is still desired for the new tool.

**Description**  
Admins would like visibility into which client versions users are running so they can identify who needs to update. When a user logs into an org, the client version is already tracked and stored by the backend. The contact list and user details views should surface this information and indicate when an update is available.

**Requirements**

- Enhance the contact list so that:  
  - Each user entry shows an “Update available” indicator if any of that user’s registered clients are on a version that can be upgraded based on the org’s current minimum/target version logic.  
  - Users with no available updates continue to display as they do today (no extra indicator).

- Enhance the user details view so that:  
  - It lists all clients registered for that user (e.g., by device name, platform, or client ID, depending on what the backend provides).  
  - For each client, display the last known client version that registered with the backend.  
  - If applicable, also show whether an update is available for each client (e.g., via a badge or status text next to the version).

- General behavior:  
  - Version and “update available” status should refresh based on the latest data sent from clients on login or periodic check‑ins.  
  - The feature should work across orgs, respecting any org‑specific version policies (minimum required, recommended, etc.).  

**Acceptance Criteria**

- Admins can see an “Update available” indicator in the contact list for any user with at least one outdated client.  
- Selecting a user from the contact list shows a details page listing all known clients and their last registered versions.  
- For a test setup with mixed client versions (some current, some outdated), the indicators and version information are accurate and consistent with the backend data.  
-

There appears to be an issue with the notification system. But more diagnosis is required.

### Steps to reproduce

2. Import the CSV
3. Sign all

items.title: Contact List Navigation Refresh
items.body:
### Problem
- Contact list doesn't refresh when navigating to/from the page, even after new users are added.
- Users must fully close and reopen the app to see newly invited organization members.

### Proposed Solution
- Add logic to refresh the contact list data whenever the contact list page is navigated to.
- Implement on-page-load and route-change handlers to fetch latest organization contacts.
- Ensure the refresh happens efficiently (e.g., only if data is stale or changed), if possible.

### Acceptance Criteria
- Navigating to contact list page always shows latest organization members.
- Navigating away from and back to contact list refreshes the data.
- Page load on contact list fetches current contacts without manual browser refresh.

items.title: Errors are hard to track
items.body: Users mentioned that the toast messages that appear after an error don't remain long enough to determine what the actual issue was. Even if the error stayed for 4 or more seconds, this may not be sufficient for a user to understand the issue. In addition to a longer toast message, these errors may need to be added to the Notification Center when implemented.

items.title: Ready to Sign loading issues
items.body:
The Ready To Sign tab does not load as quickly as it should. This is experienced when there are many transactions that are in progress (Waiting for Signatures or Waiting for Execution). 

The reason for the slow loading is that every time the front end makes a request for all transactions that the user needs to sign, the back end needs to do a lot of computing:
1. Pull each transaction that is either Waiting for Signatures or Waiting for Execution
2. Determine if the user has already signed the transaction (via transaction_signer table and also checking the signatures in the transaction bytes).
3. Determine which accounts are involved and need to sign
4. Look up the current keys for those accounts
5. Determine if the user is an owner of any of the public keys obtained from mirror node
6. Return all transactions the user needs to sign.

Because of all these required steps, the user experience is poor. The results take too long to compute.

items.title: Sign button doesn't disappear after signing
items.body:
### Problem

When signing a transaction, the sign button doesn't go away even though I got a toast message saying the transaction was signed successfully. This is caused by a connection issue with the WebSocketGateway (e.g. ERROR [WebsocketGateway] Socket client connected without user). 
As this bug allowed for a transition to be signed multiple times, it was noted that each time the transaction was signed, a new transaction was essentially created and submitted to the network. This is likely due to the transaction submitting to the network between the first signature and the second.
Due to multiple signings/transactions, the first transaction was successful, but subsequent transactions were marked as duplicate.

### Solution

The root cause of the issue needs to be addressed, as well as adding safeguards for potentially related issues.
1. Discover why the websocket did not reconnect. This specific case happened due to a user having the app open and running while the notification service pod was redeployed. Websocket should have reconnected, but doesn't appear to have done so properly.
2. Determine if a client side health check and reconnect to the server feature is possible
3. Sign button could be changed to not rely solely on the websocket notification, but also on the response to the signing of the transaction. If a transaction is signed and the server responds with a successful upload, the sign button can be hidden.
4. On the back-end, if the transaction is already being executed, prevent further signatures
5. Ensure that a transaction doesn't get 'duplicated' due to the above.
6. When duplicate transactions are found in history, either display all transactions, or at least display the transaction that is not a duplicate so that history will show that the first transaction was successful. 
7. If a transaction isn't actually duplicated, but instead has its status/response code updated due to the duplicate submission, don't allow the duplicate submission

items.title: Unintended notification for user
items.body:
### Description

When User A creates a transaction that does not include themselves (i.e., User A is not a participant in the transaction they create for User B), User A incorrectly receives a notification badge on the "Ready to Sign" tab. Upon clicking this tab, User A encounters a 404 error in the console. User A should not receive any notifications for transactions they are not a part of.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Log in as User A.
2. Create a new transaction without including yourself as a participant (only include User B).
3. Observe that a notification badge appears on the "Ready to Sign" tab.
4. Click on the "Ready to Sign" tab.
5. Check the browser console for errors.

### Additional context

**Actual result:**
User A sees a notification badge on the "Ready to Sign" tab despite not being part of the transaction.

**Expected result:**
User A should not receive a notification for transactions they are not involved in.

items.title: Provide notification for transaction creators
items.body:
### **User Story**

As a transaction creator, I want to be notified when a transaction has received its signatures, executes, or fails so I can take the appropriate action to manage my transactions.



### **Acceptance Criteria**
**Notification for Signatures Received:**

The transaction creator/observer receives a notification when the transaction has received the minimum number of signatures required for execution.
The notification message should state: Transactions ready for execution.
The notification should include a link to view the transaction details in the Ready for Execution status.

**Notification for Successful Execution:**

The transaction creator/observer receives a notification when the transaction is successfully executed on the network.
The notification message should state: Transaction executed successfully.
The notification should link to the history page when the user clicks on it.

**Notification for Transaction Failure:**

The transaction creator/observer receives a notification if the transaction fails upon execution.
The notification message should state: Transaction failed.
Clicking on the notification should navigate the user to the transaction history with details about the failure.
The new entires in history should have a badge. 
The badge is removed when the user navigates away from the history page. 

**Notification for Transaction Expiration**

The transaction creator/observer receives a notification if the transaction expires before receiving the required number of signatures.
The notification message should state: Transaction expired.
The notification should include a link to view the expired transaction details in the history tab when clicked.

**Notification Delivery**
Notifications should also be sent via email service.

**Notification Management**

The transaction creator/observer should be able to view all notifications in the notifications center.
The transaction creator/observer should be able to dismiss or mark notifications
